bettygriffin:
Betty Griffin

“We always thought Danville was the epitome, the cat’s meow.”

Parts of this transcript were autogenerated and there may be errors.

Content warning: heavy themes, strong language, mention of gun violence

Transcript

Kate Bell: Hi. My name is Kate Bell, and today I'm interviewing Dr Betty Griffin, who is a highly accomplished Danville native. I am here with Eliza Riley, Ella Vazquez, Dr. Shenton, and a small audience. Today is January 24, 2025 we are recording this interview in Danville Boyle County African American Historical Society. And today we'll be discussing Dr Griffin's experiences growing up in Danville and the rest of her career. So to start out with, just tell us about yourself, give us the rundown.

Dr. Griffin: About myself?

Kate Bell: About yourself.

Dr. Griffin: Well, I'm the older person in the room, so I've been here a while [laughs] but I did my… I'm from Danville, originally. Graduated from Bate High School here. I'm very pleased about Bate. And I served when… I retired recently from Kentucky State. I served as dean of education at Kentucky State, but I have taught at…in Philadelphia in high school, I have taught in military dependent Schools. And so basically, an educator. I'm very proud of that fact. So Danville is very proud of me as well as school. So I don't know where you're from originally, I've met some of your Danville people before that I've run into, not here, but here. So…but, I'm pleased to be here, to see what we are about to do.

Kate Bell: Wonderful. Well, maybe let's start at the beginning. Tell us about growing up in Danville a little bit.

Dr. Griffin: Well, I don't know [what] it is about Danville, that is…is an outstanding commodity, if you will. We always felt that Danville was… [we] knew more about Harrodsburg or Louisville or Lexington. We always thought Danville was the epitome, the cat’s meow. And so growing [up] here in Danville, I would see Centre College. We would see, obviously, the university here. It was ironic that the school that you now participate in, Centre, was part of my undergraduate school. I graduated [from] Fisk University in Nashville, Tennessee, and so my parents decided that's where I would go to school. No one asked me. I don't think anyone ever called me and said, “would you like to go to school?” And “we want to go to school here.” No, that was not the way that school was designed at that particular time. We were told that you were going to do certain kinds of things as you were growing up. And one of those was that when parents say you go to college, you went to college, no one asked me if I wanted to come to college. No one asked me if I wanted to come to Fisk or anywhere. They just said, “you're going to college and you're going to graduate from high school, and when you graduate, you're going to Fisk.” So it was a different kind of scenario that we used to do at this particular time. Parents didn't ask me anything. I don't remember anyone calling me up and saying, “well, you're going to be one of the outstanding people that you're going to do [be].” No one asked me that. They just said, “you're supposed to go, you graduated,” and I went. It was just that simple. I like that idea. I wish, in fact…I wish a lot of young people now knew a little bit more about what their parents had in mind. So it wasn't an idea that you would [go]for four years. You went for four years, period. It was no idea that you'd go for two years and maybe spend a year, and a half a year. No, you went for four years, and at the end of four years, the next stop…stop along the way. I asked if I wanted to go. I'm not sure that anybody asked me if I wanted to get my degree…undergraduate degree, no, they just said that. And the same thing when it came to my graduate degree. And then my doctorate is from Oregon State in Corvallis, Oregon. By that time, I realized you're supposed to go to college, but it was a wonderful scenario, as far as the way that it was designed.

Eliza Riley: Can you tell us a little bit about your family? Like your parents growing up, if you have any siblings? Anything about that?

Dr. Griffin: What about my…?

Eliza Riley: Your family?

Dr. Griffin: Well, my parents are from here in Danville. My grandparents are from Talladega, Alabama. My undergraduate, of course, my undergraduate degree… undergraduate…my degree. At that time, we were not in college. My father, grandfather and my grandmother were [was] Betsy Caldwell, and that [she] was considered to be a lady who did what she's supposed to do. And so we moved to…no one asked me that either. But we moved to Talladega, Alabama. And I would spend my summers with my two cousins. They would drive me back to Alabama for me to meet my uncles and my aunts. That was before the time of civil rights, of course. We were told where not to go in Talladega, there were certain places you were not supposed to go. And so we went. Pure and simple. And so once we finished high school, my parents, my dad, my uncles and my aunts all went up…went to high school. I was the only one that went on to college, but I was expected to do this, and so I did what they told me to do.

Ella Vasquez: Can you tell us a little bit about your experience in schooling at Bate?

Dr. Griffin: I'm sorry?

Ella Vasquez: Can you tell us a little bit about your experience being schooled at Bate?

Dr. Griffin: My going to Bate? I'm still not sure.

Ella Vasquez: Bate High School?

Dr. Griffin: Oh, in high school? Yes, here in Danville, everyone attended high school. We didn't have the thing now where a lot of kids go and stay and they drop out, and that kind of thing. Everyone was at the same school at the same time. Now, some of the young guys would go and work at the tobacco places in order to raise money, but there were no jobs here in Danville. The only job that we were asked to do were [was] working for domestic people. My grandmother worked for the mayor. That was a big thing to do at that particular time… is that the mayor's wife owned the…what was there…and so we thought, like we were more important, that we were made [making] a little bit more money than probably, we probably had, and we didn't…parents didn't make a lot of money at that time. In the summers, I would go in the… with my aunts. They work[ed] at Harrington lake, the Clays, very prominent family. Every summer, my aunt would drive me to Harrington Lake, and we'd work all summer, from the time that…time [from the time we had] to get up until they decided it was time for us to come home. But for the most part, all the domestic people…for the most part, the only person that I knew… there was one person who worked here in Danville, and she worked for Cinderella. Cinderella was a ladies' shop. Was a shop here downtown. Now she only worked with the African Americans. We were negros at that particular time. Some of you all remember that the Negro, okay, well, we were Negroes at that particular time and Miss Rosetta, if she was going to need some help, she would go to her place at Cinderella, and she would help other black people work with them, stockings and things like that. But as far as being a part of the models, no, we did not.

Eliza Riley: So you mentioned going to Fisk University, which is in Nashville. How was that like Nashville, different from Danville?

Dr. Griffin: Do that again

Eliza Riley: You mentioned going to Fisk University, which is in Nashville. How is that different from Danville? Did you notice any major changes in the city? Was there any kind of, like, culture shock, or anything going to Nashville?

Dr. Griffin: Fisk is the, as far as I'm concerned, the predominant school for… period. African Americans in particular. Very private. And as I said, again, no one asked me if I was planning on attending Fisk. The idea was, you're going to Fisk. The older I’ve gotten, the more I’ve realized how important it was to attend Fisk. Ironically enough, I run into numbers and numbers of young people who graduated from Fisk. At that particular time, Centre would have some type of an arrangement with Fisk where your basketball team or [was] comparable that [to what] we had. But yes it is a private institution, very predominant. And so yeah.

Ella Vasquez: Can you tell us a little bit about any clubs or organizations you were a part of outside of school growing up?

Dr. Griffin: I’m Sorry?

Ella Vasquez: Can you tell us a little bit about any clubs or organizations that you were a part of growing up in Danville?

Dr. Griffin: I'm still not getting you.

Ella Vasquez: Can you tell us about clubs or organizations, maybe anywhere that you went to church, even outside of school, when you were growing up in Danville? [Michael Hughes repeats question]

Dr. Griffin: Organizations?

Ella Vasquez: Yes!

Dr. Griffin: Well, one of the activities that…everyone was a part of the scenario. You belong to private clubs, you belong to…well, for the most part, private clubs. And so when there would be activities going on, then my mother may have been a member of the Orchid Girls, or her… someone in her family might be a member of the group. But we had our own organizations that we met. But no, there was nothing that has [had] to do at all with African Americans, in particular, with the attitude of you stay in your place, and I'll stay in my place. It was no attitude that [there] is [was] something wrong with that, or there's something bad with that. It's just that I knew who would…places that my mother would go, or places I would go. But for the most part, no, it was a very separate organization, separate community that we lived in. You lived here and I lived there, pure and simple. And I'm not sure that there was ever any attitude that there's something wrong with it. I never grew up thinking there was something wrong with a member of an organization here in Danville. But no, it's just a separate, totally [different] thing. It was a totally different scenario.

Kate Bell: So going back to school a little bit, I'm curious about the cultural shift between Bate and Fisk. How did that change?

Dr. Griffin: between Bate and Fisk?

Kate Bell: Uh huh.

Dr. Griffin: Well, the principal…the principal simply said, “Mr. Summers,” I assume she told that…told my mother that…that you're [I was] going to Fisk. My dad worked for the railroad, Southern Pacific Railroad, and most of the men here, they either work for the tobacco companies. They cut tobacco. They do things here. They may have been waiters or something like that, but for the most part, men worked that way, and women were domestic for the most part. But I think my principal, Mr. Summers, the principal of the high school, decided that it would be wonderful for me to attend Fisk. Kentucky State University, was the historically black college at that particular time. My mother graduated from Kentucky State, most of your teachers graduated from Kentucky State University. They were private…well public. And they decided that… someone got together and said, “Bett’s going to Fisk University.” And I didn't know at that particular time what that meant. I then, of course, when we got involved at Fisk, we knew what was going on. Private. At Fisk [there] was a medical school across the…Fisk…across the street from Fisk… only private schools. And very well to do parents tend to attend Fisk. I don't know how they got me there. I have no idea. I nearly knew that this is what I was supposed to do. And so for four years, that's what I was going to do. Some of your outstanding African American people…or decided to graduate from Fisk. I'm very proud, very proud of Fisk. I didn't know about Centre College. I knew you were across the street from us. We weren’t across the street, necessarily. But we knew Centre College was there. We stayed on Centre’s side of…I stayed on my side. So we had no kind of orientation or anything that said, let's get the white students with the black students. No, never was that ever decided. I think it just happened.

Michael Hughes: I think one of the things she was asking was the difference between Bate, leaving Bate and going to Fisk. Was that [right]? What was the…what was the big difference after…

Dr. Griffin: Well, between Bate… attending Bate. That's what you do. We didn't have the attitude that you are supposed to do certain kinds of things at Bate, or…I was involved in everything when it came to Bate. I was not a cheerleader. I was not any of [the] majorettes or any of those. I was in the band. I was part of the band members, but for the most part, we did our activities and they went to their activities. We used to tease us about our “Cracker Barrel” High school. It was so small, Bate high school, that they said the people would start getting ready because they knew how poor we are. I don't know [if] we were poor or not. The idea was that we played our basketball game, but they played their basketball games. But no, there was, there was no scenario that said, you are “this does this” at Bate, and “this does this” at…No, we don't do that. No, separate. And that was going on at any school here, when it comes to the high school, they were private, private being you were all white and we were all black, pure and simple. There was no scenario that said that someone was better than one or the other. Harrodsburg played against Bate. Bate against Stanford. They played all the black colleges, and we played all white colleges. Does that help?

Ella Vasquez: Can you tell us a little bit about your collegiate career after Fisk?

Dr. Griffin: A bit…what?

Ella Vasquez: About college after Fisk? The other universities that you attended and like your doctorate?

Michael Hughes: What you did after you left Fisk?

Dr. Griffin: When I graduated from Fisk, I was told that I was going to…I decided that I was going to go ahead and become a teacher. We didn't have any teachers here in Danville. In fact, we didn't have any! If you were black teachers or the principal, they were the only ones that were there. And so when it came down to what are you going to graduate… I know my roommate was from South Carolina, and I'd never heard of anyone from South Carolina. They didn't know I was from Danville. Most of us was [went to a] very private school. And so they told me they were going to…I think they said no one talked about going to law school. As I think back now, I think a lot had to do with the fact of not having counselors. We didn't have white counselors. We didn't have black counselors. No one, just all the teachers taught you what you're supposed to be able to learn. But as far as deciding whether or not I decide I'll go to Fisk or I would go to Meharry. Meharry was a private…still is a private college, a medical college. The one person that I knew here. There were two people that I knew here in Danville that had…are from Danville…had graduated from Fisk. But for the most part, we decided that, I decided that I was gonna go ahead and become a high school teacher. And so my husband was a Marine at that particular time, and he was at Tennessee's A&I, which is a private…which is the public school, a state school. And he decided that he was going to become a Marine. And so because of the Marine Corps, I lived in various different organizations, various different groups. As I said, I started out by teaching at Camp Lejeune in North Carolina. I was the first black teacher that…they say they were [I was] the first one that they'd ever had. And I remember the teacher calling me in and saying, “Are you all right?” And I said, “What do you mean are you all right?” She said, “Well, we don't have any black teachers.” The only black teacher that you have… at that particular time, it never occurred to me that I was supposed to do something different. I'm going to be an elementary teacher to begin with. Camp Lejeune, at that particular time, was doing Vietnam, and so we traveled throughout the country after… once we did their undergraduate degree. Now I taught…my graduate degree, undergraduate degree, was from Philadelphia. I taught in high school. You see now the TV thing called Abbott Elementary, or something very similar? In fact, that's the exact same school! That Abbott Elementary. But it's the same kind of a situation where [there are] all black children. Now, at that particular time you didn't have black children going to some schools and some…No, we all went to the same school at that particular time. So I hope that helps a bit….I'm a little… I don't hear well.

Eliza Riley: Do you have any impactful memories, whether it's like students or mentors from your time teaching?

Michael Hughes: Some teachers and stuff that you… when you [were] doing your time of teaching…somebody that really, that you really remember, stood out to you?

Dr. Griffin: I remember vividly all of my teachers. We were expected [to]. You didn't have teachers that you have now telling teachers what they're supposed to do. You were…[if] it was time for you to go in the fifth grade, you went to the fifth grade. You had the same teachers. In fact, my teachers…my father, he graduated from the same person that I did. We all went to the same school. This is African American. This is, this is Black before. My undergraduate, my graduate degree, there was no graduate at that particular time, but you went to Bate school, and all the students went to Bates school, same time. My father went there, my uncle went there. Everyone went to the same high school. And when you graduated, some of the people…my dad would get a job living in Louisville, and that was considered to be a good job. Whether or not they went on, I don't know. I do know that I was the only person in my high school graduating, that had graduated from Bate school at that particular time,

Ella Vasquez: And when you were teaching in North Carolina or in Philadelphia, or any of the universities that you taught at, was there any mentor that you had, or any student that stuck out to you, that really made an impact on your life?

Dr. Griffin: Other students?

Ella Vasquez: Like one of your students? Do you have any stories of a particular student that stuck out to you, or a story of a mentor while you were teaching that kind of helped you?

Dr. Griffin: Not necessarily.

Ella Vasquez: Okay

Dr. Griffin: No, not necessarily. One of the things I do know in North Carolina is that the students were sharecroppers. Well, we didn't have sharecroppers here in Danville. I didn't know if my parents attended sharecroppers, but I can remember the first time that… I guess I'd been there about half year, and they said, “all the children, all your children are going to be with Mr. X, Y and Z.” And I said, “go where? These are all black children? And they said, “well, they're Mr. So and So's children. They're sharecroppers.” And I was trying to explain [ask] to the students “Sharecropping what? What? What's a sharecropper?” And they said, “well, Mr. So and So would take all the children, and you all wouldn’t have to worry about that. You have less time than the others, and your children would be attending sharecroppers.” And it never…that's the first time I've ever seen in my life where you're supposed to take your children, everybody in your class, and leave. But that was the way that the scenario was designed. Designed that way, and it said that Mr. So and So, he gets a sharecropper of X, Y and Z, and so your children would be attending those. I learned a lot from North Carolina, because we did not have that kind of a situation here in Danville. We had a lot of people that did work in tobacco fields. As I said, you didn't have a lot of people that were working at that time for the railroad. As I said, my dad worked for the railroad, Southern Pacific Railroad, and they would go wherever the train would go. But as far as planning on going to sharecroppers, or you're going to this type of truck, we didn't have that in North Carolina. No, I, we had no counselors. Now, you probably laugh about that now, but we didn't have counselors. All the teachers are expected to be counselors. I expected my teacher to tell me what to do or to get me in trouble with whatever. But we didn't have counselors. And for many of you that are here now, you didn't have counselors as well. Teachers were teachers. You were not designed to be a specific type of teacher, but what a teacher needs to do is whatever a child needs to have help with, and that was designed for Danville. For most of the black teachers here in Danville, as I said, most of my teachers…all my teachers, for the most part, graduated from undergraduate school at Kentucky State University, my mother decided that I was going to Kentucky State [Fisk]. I don't know why. No one asked me about that. They simply said, “you're going to Fisk. You're going to Kentucky State [Fisk],” I said, “but I thought I went to Kentucky State?” “No, you're going to Fisk.” So parents decided, then, what they wanted their children to be able to have, some type of attitude about that. So that's how I was involved with Fisk. But no one asked me if I preferred to go or, no, I did not.

Kate Bell: So you mentioned a lot of people kind of told you what to do. When do you think you broke away from what was expected of you and kind of decided to do your own thing?

Dr. Griffin: I'm not sure I ever did. The question is, do I do what parents tell you [me] to do? During that era, in that particular era, parents [children] did what parents told you to do. They didn't ask you to do that. They simply said, “you are going to this college or you are not going to that college.” But parents did not sit down at the dinner table and decided that it would be nice for the child to tell them what to do. Children were not [to be] seen and not heard. We were not designed to do…and I suspect in many ways, the white students are [were] in the same situation that we were in, but I happen to know that there was no talking to parents while you are discussing [at] the dinner table. You don't do that. That's for adults. Children are to be heard and not seen [seen and not heard]. I heard something yesterday, in fact, Nashville. I saw where they had a most recent shooting in Nashville. And, you know, I thought about that. We never thought about going to the cafeteria and having a gun and deciding, you’re going to shoot this person, going to shoot that person. No. Was not, not that way. Even while Fisk is in Nashville, I attended my student teaching at Pearl high school in Nashville, right across the street from Fisk. And we did what the teachers told us to do. You were given your assignments when you first graduated, and they told you you're going to take this class, that class, that class. Children didn't decide, like now, what classes you decide to take. How did you know what classes to take? In fact, a lot of what happens with students now, as I've observed, heard, is that they don't understand what classes to take. They just decide, “I'll take this, or I'll take that, or my friend took that as well.” No, we had this situation where everybody was given a certain class. You got, everybody's going to take the same classes, period. And we don't decide, “okay, well, then I'll switch this off. I think I'll give this up to my friend.” No, no, no. Children [Adults] made a decision about where they wanted their children to spend their time, and it made it very easy. It made it very easy for you to attend high school or college, because we knew exactly what classes to take and what not to take. So you didn't have a lot of people taking classes. Just decide I'm gonna take this and next semester I'm gonna take next semester. I see that now going on. I saw it at Oregon State University. Oregon State was very similar in Corvallis, Oregon. And a lot of students are still walking around saying, “I don't know what I want to graduate,” and they've been schooled for four years already, but, “well, I don't know. Maybe I'll take this, or maybe I'll take the other,” but we had a counselor kind of organization that said “you are going to do this or you're going to do that particular way.”

Eliza Riley: So has teaching always been your passion? Like, is that what you always wanted to do growing up?

Dr. Griffin: To be a teacher?

Dr. Griffin: I only wanted to be a teacher. I thought teachers were the most important. I still do. [I think] that the most important persons [people] are the teacher[s]. We see the teachers. We attend a college. There they are [at] churches. We see our teachers at the games and things that are going on, they made it a community, kind of an organization. So no, it wasn't an idea that you decided to do that. That's the way it was designed [unintelligible].

Ella Vasquez: Kind of switching gears.This project that we're doing is about urban renewal. So we were wondering if that was ever brought up at home or growing up, or if you have any stories that you'd like to share about that?

Michael Hughes: Your experiences with urban renewal?

Dr. Griffin: From what standpoint, urban renewal?

Ella Vasquez: Like the street that you grow up on, any differences or changes that you saw?

Dr. Griffin: No.

Ella Vasquez: No?

Dr. Griffin: Not necessarily.

Michael Hughes: Were you affected? Were your family affected? Were you or your family affected by urban renewal?

Dr Griffin: We were, we were strongly affected by urban renewal. Yes, when Centre College, when Danville decided that they are going to do certain kinds of things in this community, that meant that a lot of black people lost their jobs, they lost their communities. A lot of things have happened, and because of Danville, because of urban renewal. A Lot of people gave up their jobs, or they lost their jobs, or they could not afford to keep their jobs. I think it was a bad idea initially, but I think the idea was, “let's get the people separated” and that… Centre College now, at one time, where you are in school now, all of that was the black community. That's…one whole side of that community belonged to black people. And suddenly we started, we discovered that black people had to give up their jobs, they had to move. Who made those decisions? And of course, I would not be in any kind of position to make that [claim], but I can say this: is that we lost a lot of what we now know because of urban renewal. The intent…the intent was, “let's get these things better organized, better combined with the people involved.” I'm not sure that that was the best idea. In fact, I know it wasn't the best idea. But black people did certain kinds of things. We had certain kinds of areas that were decided: “that's for black people,” and then we're going to do certain kinds of other things. I don't think urban renewal was in our best interest at the time. At the time. I think there was much going on at that particular time that would help black people.

Kate Bell: So what about the other places that you lived and worked? Did you see any of that community dividing in other places, too?

Dr Griffin: By all means.

Kate Bell: Tell me about that.

Dr Griffin: By all means, that has to do with civil rights. When I graduated from Fisk, I was a sophomore when Martin Luther King Jr. came through Nashville. Jesse Jackson was at the same time that I was, in that same era. We marched at that time. We were concerned about what was going on and what was not going on when it comes to African Americans, and we did not like what was going on. So I marched. And they explained to me very carefully. “You could get put out of college, get put out of college, out of Fisk.” Well, if one person was going to go, everybody else is going to go. So we all went at the same time. But yes, I was in Nashville in 1960… when Martin Luther King Jr., Jesse Jackson…in fact, most of our teachers at that particular time were in school, in college, and we all were getting…trying to get civil rights, because we knew exactly what was going on and what was not going on. And what was not going on was doing anything to help black people. Most of the people who attended Fisk at that since… since years, [time] have decided they now are in Nashville, in New Orleans, or different other places, but for the most part, were from Nashville. We came from Nashville for the most part. But yes, civil rights was something that was inherent. And I can remember one of my teachers here in Danville, she was asking me if I thought it was all right. Well, no one asked that. Excuse me, no teacher ever asked me that. They simply said, “Betty Sue, are you aware of the fact that you are involved with civil rights?” And I said, “Yes, my classes are… [i’m] taking classes there.” She said, “Be careful. Be very careful. Make sure that you're not by yourself at night.” And we were always together when it came to anything going on, because civil rights was something… John Lewis was in my class at that particular time. He was a year older than I. And so at that particular time when we saw that John Lewis was being killed or had been beat up the way he was, we [it] made us more involved in civil rights, but there were people who did not want civil rights, and they did everything they still do. They still do that today. Please understand that. That this is not a situation that means we're going to decide what we're going to do. No, there were certain kinds of people who took it upon themselves and said, “we are going to make sure that we're going to do something for our children.” Everything was designed with the children in mind, even if your children did not attend high school or college. Everyone's concerned about their children, because we [they] are the future. We were concerned that we were not allowing things to happen that had happened to them. So as I said, my parents are from Talladega, Alabama, and my parents talked about that to each other. Not to each other now [not to us], because, again, adults [children] will be heard, be heard and not seen. We [You] are not going to take it upon yourself to sit at a dinner table and have a discussion with your aunt and your uncle. No, no adults stay with adults and certain kinds of children, and that keeps our future clear. We make the situation much clearer than it is now.

Eliza Riley: Did you notice any major differences in Danville between living here in your childhood and coming back after college?

Dr. Griffin: What about it?

Eliza Riley: Did you notice any major changes in the community of Danville, versus your childhood and coming back after college?

Dr. Griffin: I did not come back to come back… to college [I did not come back right after college]. I had, as I said, I was, I was involved with the Marine Corps, and so I knew I was traveling around the country as they were. But did we come to Danville? No, we never thought about the idea of, let's come home to do something. We would see coming home from church. We would see coming home to certain kinds of things going on, we would celebrate each other. But as far as deciding to come and do something or…no, I think that I was one of the few students, and I may be wrong, that went on to get a doctorate. In fact, I'm sure I was the only person that got a doctorate here in Danville. And everyone was celebrating the idea of that, but never to come back to Danville. There's nothing going on in Danville that was going to be anything that was going to allow us to continue on to the future.

Eliza Riley: Can you tell us a little bit about your experience while getting your doctorate? Like, what was that…

Dr. Griffin: Like what?

Eliza Riley: About getting your doctorate? Like, what was that level of education like at the time?

Dr. Griffin: The same level as any other education when it comes with a PhD. Is that…I was teaching in Oregon, and I was given an opportunity to attend, and I didn't think at the time that it was something I wanted to do, but once I got to Corvallis and I started to see what was going on in other schools, I said, “wonderful.” I think if I would have done this differently, I would have decided to go to law school because I'd had the teaching in mind. But I still had a wonderful opportunity, as far as Corvallis is concerned, as far as getting a PhD, as far as the type of materials I had to take, in fact, a lot of it…I was it. I was the person, the black person here in Corvallis. They had finally found somebody that wanted to be a teacher or wanted to do something, and I was treated very well. I'm not going to tell you something differently. I was treated very nicely in Corvallis. But I was teaching more. A lot of people did not expect that to happen. They assumed that black people would automatically do certain kinds of things. And so I was over the Black Student Union. I worked with a group [of] young people there. Anytime there was something to do with black children, I was involved with it, when it came to Corvallis. I lived in Portland, off and on and so, but I got an opportunity to do that as well, but for the most part, a doctorate. Yes, I thought it was necessary to have happen. It was absolutely vital for you [me] to go ahead and finish.

Eliza Riley: What was your dissertation about? To receive your doctorate? Your dissertation? To receive your PhD, what was it about?

Dr. Griffin: Say it again.

Eliza Riley: Your dissertation to receive your PhD. What was that about?

Dr. Griffin: Basically about education, and it had to do with…when it came to combining… At that particular time, I graduated from Oregon State. I've been here a while now, I'll be perfectly honest with you. But at that particular time, it’s that we were concerned about the country, about what's going on around the country, and whether or not our people, the African Americans, were going to be actively involved in the John Lewis's of the world, or the Jesse Jackson's of the world, or what was going on around the world. But as far as particular things in mind…yes. I was constantly involved with African American students. Always have been and always will be, because a lot of things that go on. Young people assume it happened because you went that way. No, that's not true. And so I have used my particular document and my degrees and most of the people that I've worked with… In fact, I have taught as recently as this morning, that someone called and wanted to know if I were to do a class for them, and so I'm constantly doing something in order to get people involved with them.

Kate Bell: So I don't mean to jump ahead, but I am curious, as such an accomplished changemaker, I'm curious about your thoughts about activism today? And kind of what goes into that, what important values make…

Dr. Griffin: Activism and what?

Kate Bell: Activism and changemaking and movements today?

Dr. Griffin: This is probably, maybe because of the President, maybe because of the inherent things that's going on now… that I would feel very uncomfortable as young people now making the kind of decisions that are being made. I say that to say when it comes to African Americans and whether or not they are involved… We said something recently, back last week, we were concerned about…they've decided that there's going to be… the country is going to decide that we're going to be in a country that is predominantly white, period. That the people in the world, they want white people to do things for all white people, and then we'll decide whether or not we'll allow other people to get involved. I'm very concerned about that, because I'm concerned about the young people involved. It’s that… I'm not certain that you are [as] involved as you should be when it comes to what should happen with this country. If someone asked you tomorrow, you said, “well, I voted” and “yes, I voted for Trump” or “I voted for Biden” or for Kamala or whomever. I'm not certain that our young people have made the kind of decision with adults in mind to think about what's going to happen in this country. This is your country. This is our country. But the idea and the attitude that this is going to be designed and we'll have certain kinds of people in mind, and there'll be other kinds of people in mind. I say that to say, as recently as yesterday, we were talking about this, and it was concerned about if the Jews decided that they're going to take over…then what's going to happen with the Jews? Someone says, “Well, so? But what are we going to do with the African Americans?” What's next? In line for you? Are we in line as African American in order to be in kind of a decision, a decision that will make a change in the world? I'm not certain that that's true. I'm not certain that our young people are as well prepared, as they should be, to take over this country and be prepared for the mothers and the fathers and the young people going along. We're going to have a very horrible situation, I believe, in mind of what's going on because of what's going on right now. Be very careful about what is happening in this country. It's not a matter of just walking around Centre’s campus and seeing someone or you happen to be this person or that person. No, the attitude is, are we really prepared to take over this country? I'm not certain. I'm certain that we're…I know we're certain that we are not in a position to be able to take over, that we need to take over. So yes, I would share that in mind, to be very careful. Because I think we're about to get into a scenario that is going to have us… to lose a lot of people, and we're going to give people…[unintelligible]. People are going to be hurt. People are going to be killed. This is not just every other day and you…but you voted, and then it's going to be, everything's going to be alright. No, it's not going to be alright. What really would you be prepared to do? I don't know what your college professors are… I know that one or two here in Centre, at Centre…but you don't have enough college professors at Centre or even at UK. I spent a sabbatical doing a… UK and of course, Dr Singletary was concerned about what was going on when it came to University of Kentucky, so I spent one year sabbatical. To see what was going on at UK. UK was as far behind us when it came to African Americans as they were when we were here in 1985. We did not think about that. And so if we continue to go the way we are going, you're going to lose a lot of young people, you're going to lose a lot of people who say no, just because you happen to [be] African American means that this is going to happen. That's not going to happen that easily.

Kate Bell: So as you know, our project is focused very heavily on Danville, and I am curious about your perspective growing up in Danville. What are some of your memories of Second Street specifically?

Dr. Griffin: I look at the picture right behind you as I look back on that. Second street, this street, is considered to be a social kind of event. This is where people came. My uncle and my father, stepfather, owned the barber shop and the cars and things that are here in Danville. And we have to make a decision that there's certain kinds of people who… they work on this side of town, or they don't work on this side of town. But when, as far as Danville is concerned, Danville has become a totally different scenario when it comes to what Danville should have turned itself into being. Right now it's more or less a nice thoroughfare that we walk through and we see people, and once a year we get together and we have this and then we leave ourselves alone. No, I don't think that that is what we had in mind when it came to Danville. But Danville has changed considerably. It will continue to change and in some ways, wonderfully, but there are some ways and some things in Danville that we want to make sure that we be very careful about Danville. Because it's very private and it is basically, it is a private community. If you think about Danville and you think about Harrodsburg or Lexington or Louisville, totally different things.

Ella Vasquez: We've talked a lot about loss of African American communities here in Danville. Can you talk a little bit about what you hope the future will look like, maybe getting that community back?

Dr. Griffin: What position?

Ella Vasquez: We've talked a lot about the loss of African American communities here in Danville, and I was wondering your perspective on the future and how maybe you would hope to see that community come back?

Dr. Griffin: Because of Danville, and not just Centre College, but you have your colleges, your…My mother worked for a community here or a factory here in Danville. Most of our people now are able to get out and get into certain areas and in order to combine themselves with that. But as far as Danville is as a community, the main thing that Centre College was concerned about is how Centre has taken over what it had designed to do. That much of what is Centre College is now what used to be African American communities. There are people who are not that happy about that. And I could understand, if I lived here in Danville, I would be concerned. My stepfather, he is, he doesn't come anywhere near Centre, because a lot of the communities are different than Centre was at that particular time, and so a lot of people in Danville at Centre, as far as it's concerned, it's almost as though it's a different, different place totally. You get a particular professor or two professors at Centre and then we are supposed to be the only people that are involved with Centre. Centre College could be more involved. If we're going to be about Centre College, if we're going to be about preparing young people to come to Centre, you need more than just a professor here or professor there. And so we need to get more of your professors involved in what's going on. You need Asian professors. You need other types of professors. One of the advantages that I had was because there were so many people in Corvallis and University of Oregon and Oregon State that we met people from all different [places, different] kinds of professors. Centre, for the most part, Danville, for the most part, is a very closed, private [place], as was Fisk when I was in school. It was designed that way. So we need to open up a little bit about what's going on when it comes to Centre College. Did we get along? Of course, we got along. There was no question about getting along. But the question is, what are we doing to the students who come? Who really knows the students, who knows anything about Centre? I don't know. I really don't, but I like the idea that you're doing, from the standpoint of checking and seeing what is going on at Centre College.

Kate Bell: We want to know more about your family barber shop. What was that like?

Dr. Griffin: Right across the street from this. My stepfather owned the barber shop, owned the taxi place. I wouldn't know because women would not even step on that side of the street. Women did not walk on the side of the street. When we would leave to go to Sunday school, every Sunday, you could come across the street and you would go to the supply store, and we would get ice cream, and then we would turn around and go back. But ladies did not walk on the sidewalk. No, they did not. You're not going to find women going to barber shops and things like that. We did not do that kind of thing. That was, there are certain kinds of people who do certain kinds of things. But no, we are not involved. We were not involved with that. As far as the taxis are concerned, my stepfather owned the taxis, and so I think there were five or six of them that owned cars. And black people were drivers, drove certain kinds of people. White people very seldom drove other people. If you, if you understand the way that the South was designed at that particular time, and really is, it really is, but at that particular time is that–this is your community and that's your community, and you keep those two separate, pure and simple. Every now and then, guys would sneak out and go with black girls, black girls, black white guys would go with white girls. But we knew about what was going on, but you could be killed that way for doing that. So it wasn't an open kind of community where you come and involve that and enjoy yourself. No, no. Parents were, let me say this again. Parents are very concerned about what's going to happen to the their futures, and they want to make sure that those, that your children are not going to get involved in situations that's going to cause them some kind of a, any kind of a situation that's going to cause them [to] lose their job, lose their parents, lose that kind of thing.

Ella Vasquez: You've talked a lot about the importance of parents and their role in their children's lives. Do you have any children?

Dr. Griffin: No, I did not have children, unfortunately

Ella Vasquez: Okay.

Dr. Griffin: But what is it?

Ella Vasquez: Oh, just asking if you had any children.

Dr. Griffin: No, as a school teacher, I had children everywhere I taught, from Camp Lejeune to North Carolina to Oregon to Philadelphia. I've taught all over the country, but no, I did not have children of my own. But again, the counselor kind of thing. While we didn't have the actual counselor of a child, every child was considered, every teacher is considered to be an ambassador to make sure that our children attend school.

Eliza Riley: Do you have a favorite age group to teach? Do you have a favorite age group of children to teach?

Dr. Griffin: hmm?

Eliza Riley: A favorite age group” Like, do you prefer teaching younger kids, older kids?

Michael Hughes: A favorite age group of people you like to teach?

Dr Griffin: I don't think that there's any one… small or the other… less than, when it comes to being involved with young people, it's all about academics. It's about, “were you able to be able to do that? Am I able to help you get something in whatever it is that you desire in mind?” But no, it’s…I don't think it's about age group. I don't think that in particular. In fact, I'm afraid that we don't have enough young teachers right now, in order to allow us to do that.

Dr. Shenton: Oh, you said your uncle and your stepfather owned the barber shop. What were their names? Just for the record.

Dr. Griffin: The Richardsons! They're right behind you. The Richardsons, [that] was my stepfather. They owned, the barber shop and the pool hall and all of the businesses that's on that particular… on second street and so that my stepfather lived on that. I mean, that's where they work.

Dr. Shenton: Thank you for that. So we've had you for about an hour. Is there anything else that we haven't talked about, that you would like to tell the audience here today? Or anything else we haven't talked about?

Dr. Griffin: I think in general, the whole thing is about expectations. You have made some expectations about what you think is going to happen with Centre College, that this is going to make a major difference. I remember coming here recently, within the last five years. She and about five young girls, African Americans, who have come to Boston and to different places here at Centre. And they are… in order to get involved in Centre. Centre College was not as many people involved as they should be, and I got to be involved with Ariel and a lot of their students who were involved at Centre. But as far as that's concerned, yes, we would, in order, would take Ariel with me to church. I would take Ariel with me to Louisville, or I would take Ariel to Lexington, because she was a white student, she's a black student. And she felt like she was one person in general. And there would be other black students… There were 10 black students from your university, and they came to Boston and to different places here, and they were trying to expand the African American population. We did not do as well as we should have when it came to getting them involved, but for the most part, kids from Centre stay with Centre. Very seldom do you see any kind of an area that gets them involved with each other. And we need to do that. The same thing happened when I was at Fisk. It was designed that way.

Dr. Shenton: Michael, did you have anything you wanted to add?

Michael Hughes: Yeah, I will. I want to do something of a summation with you know, I wanted to know how many, I don't know, how many interviews we've done and been done since we started, probably…

Dr. Shenton: Seven now. Overall, we've done almost 40 total, but we had seven.

Michael Hughes: This particular class had seven and talked to several different people. I want to, just to see what has changed your thinking, what you have learned from the interviews of the different, different… anybody that…I'll just take you three young ladies here. What have you since the first interviews, what have you learned?

Ella Vasquez: I think most of the time, the interviews kind of just really emphasized how impactful in a negative way, urban renewal was on the community, for Danville, specifically the African American community. And I think like hearing the personal stories from all the people that we've interviewed, really, like put it into perspective, and like, made me understand it more than I did at the beginning.

Eliza Riley: Yeah, I mean, similar thing. I knew what urban renewal was, but I never realized how much of an impact it had on people. And so learning about it in Danville, and hearing the stories, I went and then looked about like urban renewal in my hometown. I'm from Birmingham, Alabama, so I went and looked about…sorry [laughs]... about a bunch of urban renewal projects in there, and learned more about the history of my hometown that I didn't know about before and hadn't ever looked into before.

Kate Bell: I think, for me, in addition to what they were saying about learning about urban renewal, I think too it's helped me form ideas about identifying injustices in the future, and kind of like navigating changemaking moving forward. I think the project is important, is an important start collecting those stories. But I think everything is cyclical, and even though urban renewal was in the past, something like it is bound to happen again. And I think, like, learning these stories has helped me kind of form an opinion about the importance of, I don't know, gathering these things and knowledge.

Michael Hughes: I think, I think one of the things that you probably found out, the division that was there in the early days, how they would, Danville, in particular, was black, and then that was black and white, and how the struggle it was for equality to bring… that was one of the things that was more, most important for me. Because I think we have had the…we are going on 11 plus years as an organization. Where were you at 11 years ago?

Ella Vasquez: I was, uh, elementary school [laughs]

Michael Hughes: Most of you guys were in elementary, but at the same time, you're learning now what it was like. It was the same thing that was 11 years ago, when you didn't know those same things that already had happened. Urban renewal had already happened. You're now seeing how it affected this community and all the other communities. And I, of course, I didn't live in the other communities. I grew up seeing urban renewal’s destruction, you know. And I often wonder, you know, if urban renewal had.. What urban renewal? When you go to the word renewal, you know, urban renewal. We come in here to renew, to make this better. But the accident came, and you… Do you remember when I first, when you guys first came and I spoke, and [used] a word, I used removal. You remember me saying the word removal? That is what actually happened. And it was planned. It was not, it was not something that happened as it went forward. It was already planned. It was already planned to deconstruct community and build it in the way. And I often wonder… and another word I knew was “they took the dirt.” I think that I often wonder if they would have it…. I'm going to say they removed the buildings there on Second Street, but they could have built them somewhere else. They could have… the neighborhoods, the houses that they removed, they could have built them somewhere else in Danville. So what I'm saying is that that wasn't really the plan. So I hope from… I think in 2019 when Dr. Shenton came to Danville our… I understand when you came to them, when we first met, I think he himself didn't exactly know exactly what it was all about. You know, when we met, we worked together, and I appreciate you guys coming and being attentive on everything you know everything. And I want to thank Doctor [Griffin] coming down today. You know, she's she, she has been styled in our community for so many years, you know. And she's an example of what you can do when you, when they try to hold you back. You know, she would not be held back, even though she said she didn't know. She just did what her parents told her she had to do, you know, but she didn't stop there. She went on.

Dr. Griffin: Let me something just come to mind that I think, Michael, is important for you to remember. We talk about what could have happened and what should have happened and what kinds of things actually happened. My mother was a very quiet person. She worked at Corning Glass work. She was a school teacher by training, and I can remember her. One beat, if you will. In order for black girls, and to make some money, they babysit with other people. They would bring their children to their home, and they took care of them. And I can remember the black cab, black taxi coming up to the house and telling my mother, get your things together. We're not going to stay here. I didn't know what they were talking about. What had happened is the white lady that I was hired as a, as a, babysitter [for]. They had decided that I was going to mop the front porch. Weather, this weather [was] like it is here. It was so cold, and I can remember her coming up saying that “you're going home.” And I didn't understand what that was about. The idea was, you're not going to work with this lady. Black people were hired, the young elect, the young lady to serve as babysitter. Excuse me, babysitter. And my mother said, “you are not going to work for that person.” So even though there were things going on, black people were very dignified when it came to “you're not going to teach my child differently. You're not going to teach them just less than what they should have learned,” and, but my mother said, “Mrs. Blunt, don't worry. She won't be here anymore. She won't be work… working for you any longer.” And I think it was my very first time seeing my mother making a decision about no. White people didn't have to do that, but my mother made a decision that you are not going to dignify yourself in order to do this. I'm sorry. That's all I have.

Dr. Shenton: Thank you. Dr Griffin

Dr Griffin: Thank you so much. Thank you.
ennistillman:
Ennis Tillman

“The first time I heard the word engineer, I thought about a train, but I soon learned quickly, hey, no, all engineers are not train related.”

Transcript

00:03 (Zeke) Hello. My name is Zeke Saviello, and today I'm interviewing Mr. Ennis Tillman, who moved to Danville in 1976 and has been living here since. I'm here with Drew Davidson, Jacob Sponel and Beckett Bond. Today is January 15, 2025. We are recording this interview in the Danville African American Historical Society. Today, will be discussing Mr. Ennis Tillman's experiences during urban renewal and anything else that you would like to discuss.

00:33 (Drew) Yeah. So first, I think we want to talk about your upbringing. And so I guess we're just curious about, you know, your experience growing up in Louisiana, and anything that sticks out to you?

00:49 (Ennis) Well, really, let me think about my background. I'm one of 13 children. My grandmother was a sharecropper, and I'm from Louisiana– Toulouse, Louisiana. I graduated from McCall High School, Louisiana, and I'm a Southern University graduate with a BSME, which is a professional science and mechanical engineering and pretty much athletics was my, I guess, way to basically get out of the rural area. So I was blessed to go to school on a basketball scholarship as well as academic scholarship. And my first job after graduating from college, I worked for John Deere Harvester in East Moline, Illinois for roughly six months. And the reason I left, it was too cold in Illinois. I lived in Davenport, Iowa, and then I was blessed to get a job with General Electric in my home base, Dallas, Texas. But guess what? He sent me to Schenectady, New York, which was colder than Iowa. So I was there for one and a half years and didn't like traveling a lot, so I ended up working with Whirlpool, which was located here in Danville, Kentucky. I worked for Whirlpool for 15 and a half years, and that company was purchased by Panasonic. So I finished my career with Panasonic, which was 25 years. So I basically worked at the same facility for roughly 42 years, and I retired in 2016 as a mechanical engineering manager. And we built home appliance products, vacuum cleaners, all the sears and roombas. Vacuum cleaners was purchased when I purchased what actually manufactured here in Danville, Kentucky, and they sold the business to what relocated the business to China, and that was my responsibility, to relocate the whole operation from Danville, Kentucky to China, kind of secretly before it was announced to the public. And I am a member of First Baptist Church located at 200 West Water Street here in Danville, Kentucky, been a member since 19 well, for 49 years, really, years since I moved to Kentucky, and I was blessed to join the church where It was just like my church in my hometown.

03:22 Very energetic, and if you want to get involved, it was easy to get involved to make a contribution to the community. So I'm a trustee at my church, and I teach Sunday school also to young men aged 13 to 18, and this was a nice place to raise my two children. Had two daughters. Coached them from the time they was 12 until they actually got out of high school. So I was in a high school arena for 18 years coaching basketball.

04:00 Two years I coached at Boyle County, so I got a total like 20 years of coaching basketball, and which was a contribution I could give back to the community, using my experiences as far as mentoring kids. And it's something I enjoy doing. I enjoy working with young men, because I know how I grew up. I was a mentor when I was an eighth grader, and was basically encouraged to pursue engineering degree. The first time I heard the word engineer, I thought about a train, but I soon learned quickly, hey, no, all engineers are not train related. So it was a young man took a sabbatical from Shell Oil Company in Baton Rouge, Louisiana, and took me under his wings and basically mentored me as to what need to be done to become an engineer, and he exposed me to all the different facets. So I chose the mechanical engineering field, and I've been retired since 2016

05:15 (Drew) So it seems pretty apparent that you really like to give back to the community, whether it be through basketball or religion, whatever the case may be. So would you say that you enjoy doing that because you had such a great mentor growing up? Or you enjoy doing it because there was such a lack of mentor for you. Why, Why do you think that you know you really enjoy giving back?

05:49 (Ennis) I enjoy giving back because it's one of my passions, and I did. I was a mentor, so I figured I should give back to the community. And I see so many teaching Sunday school. I see a bunch of young men today that coming from single parent homes, and at the same time, being a young man, you know you need to be exposed to things that you don't normally get exposed to, like job shadowing. There are many ways to become somewhat independent, but you need to be exposed to those opportunities. So I try to inform guys, there's other ways to survive, other than going to college. You know, you can go to trade school, become your own Entrepreneur on your own business, but you got to build that foundation and prepare yourself to do those things.

06:51 (Drew) Yeah, that's great. Going back to your education background, were you always intrigued with school? Did it take time to sort of become into school and enjoy learning, or is that just kind of your natural, natural way?

07:14 (Ennis) A little of both. I pride myself in being different, and I pride myself in being the best. You know, if I can make 120 on a quiz, I want to make 120 and yes, I studied hard, and I like to challenge myself. If someone tell me I can't do something, I try to prove them wrong.

07:41 (Drew) So you mentioned having 13 siblings–12, siblings I mean. Obviously that's not the average amount of siblings to have, so how was that? How was that experience growing up, and how did that, you know, sort of affect your upbringing?

08:02 (Ennis) It was very enjoyable. You know, I never went hungry a day in my life. I never went without. To me today, family is everything, and having eight brothers, which is a baseball team and for cheerleaders, it was awesome. And growing up, I didn't have to worry about being bullied because I had enough siblings to take up for me. Yeah, and likewise, we would take up for each other. And on a Sunday, it's like, if you wasn’t at the table, when the meal was put on the table, you may not get what you actually wanted to get. You know, like, it's only so many pieces of chicken, a person with chicken, so the person that wasn't at the table usually got the scripts, the leftovers, but it happens, it's just fun. It was fun growing up.

08:54 (Drew) So would you say that– well, we've talked to people before you and in class and kind of learned that oftentimes in the black community, you know, a neighborhood is like a family. Would you say that you grew up in a neighborhood that was very close knit? Or would you say that most of your community came from your large family?

09:21 (Ennis) The neighborhood, yeah, it was like the village raised the child. Yeah, yes, and that's where I grew up. For instance, if my mom needed a cup of sugar, it was nothing for her to send one of us over to the neighbor's house and borrow a cup of sugar. That's just the way it was. You know, we all helped each other, yes.

09:48 (Drew) I guess going back to basketball a little bit, you know, we're all athletes interviewing you, and we obviously have our own experience with sport. How did, how would you say that, you know, affected you growing up? How did that help you learn life skills?

10:13 (Ennis) You know, it was awesome, because, like I say, I played basketball in high school, was blessed to get a basketball scholarship, and you don't meet strangers on a, say, athletic field. It's always challenging. You may think you the best at your school, but there's always somebody better. So again, it was challenging. You know, I played center at 6 '3'’ and a half, and I thought I was the biggest kid in the area, but when we played in a state tournament, I ended up watching Robert Parish play basketball. This dude was seven feet tall, and I ended up guarding a guy that played for LSU. You probably heard of Pete Maravich, right? I played against him. It wasn't fun, and they had four guys on a team that was actually, say, 6’8’’ or better. So that's where life is. You're gonna be challenges, but you got to know how to handle those.

11:13 (Drew) So speaking of challenges, specifically with sport, do you think being Black had a major impact on how you went about playing sports, or the dynamic between you and you know, competition or teammates?

11:31 (Ennis) The answer is no. My mom always taught me, from day one, all of our blood is red, so the color of your skin has nothing there but your upbringing, your outcome. There were some challenges, but I never looked at it.

11:53 (Sponel) Obviously, growing up in Louisiana, do you think other people in the south when you were playing sports had that same mentality, or did you face some, like, adversity dealing with white athletes when you were playing basketball?

12:08 (Ennis) No, I never had any kind of negative adverse effect dealing with anybody really, and like I say, the guys that I played with, I think they felt the same way. As a matter of fact, the high school I played basketball on, we won 86 games and won three consecutive state championships, and two of the guys played in the NBA that was on my basketball team.

12:39 (Beckett) Okay, so I would like to talk about what brought you to Danville a little bit. You mentioned that you worked for Whirlpool, and that was what brought you to the city. Would you mind telling us about how you found that employment opportunity and what it was like working here once you actually moved?

12:57 (Ennis) Yes, prior to my graduation at Southern University, I had gone on probably 14 to 15 interviews, and of course, I was looking at how much money I could make when I graduated. So the biggest offer I had was with John Deere Harvester, which was like I say, East Moline, Illinois, considering the climate in Illinois or Iowa, it just was too cold, and that's where I spent the six months. So I put my resume out, and I was blessed again to get a filler from G.E. and

13:41 within two days, I was moving from Davenport, Iowa to Schenectady, New York. And coming here to Danville, it was so different. It's like, I think Dan was like an extension of a family. The community just kind of tight knit. And the big thing about Danville, people don't realize this. I guess in the south most neighborhoods are separated by railroad tracks, but here in Danville, those railroad tracks don't divide Danville. And I enjoyed raising my kids here in Danville. There was afforded opportunities that I didn't have when I was growing up, and both of my daughters went to school on scholarships. My younger daughter played basketball for the University of Kentucky for two years. She played for Bernadette Locke. My older daughter ran track for the University of Louisville. She ended up being a chiropractor.

14:49 (Jacob) We spoke with James Hunt earlier this week, and he noted that it was hard for him to make friends within the African American community that was here in Danville when he moved here. You actually mentioned that you felt really included. But was that immediate, or did it take a little time for them to get accustomed a new family?

15:07 (Ennis) For me, it was immediate. You know, I moved in town. I met some guys. As a matter of fact, I moved here on a Friday, and my mom has always told me said, ‘Boy, wherever you go, make sure you take your letter of transfer from a church.’ So I joined First Baptist on Sunday. So I was here two days and joined First Baptist Church. So I met some young men my age. They were all athletes, too, at the church. So it's pretty easy for me.

15:42 (Jacob) Did you feel that your job affected integration into the community, or was it purely from the church community that you found your– you spending time with that? If that makes sense.

15:57 (Ennis) Repeat that question again.

15:58 (Jacob) So obviously you've mentioned the impact that the church and religion has had. Did you find yourself, you know, hanging out with anyone from work, or was it purely from the church community?

16:09 (Ennis) Both, both.Whirlpool had a softball team and a basketball team, so it was pretty easy for me to make friends, because I immediately joined both of those teams. So we used to travel out of the state of Kentucky, Ohio and Tennessee and play softball. And Whirlpool Softball team won the state championship in 1996 and 98.

16:36 (Beckett) When you moved here to Danville, were there any specific places that you were like truly drawn to?

16:48 (Ennis) This church. That's me. I'm a family person.

16:53 I put God first, and I don't hang out with negative people. So everybody I hung out with positive people, even though some of those guys felt as though that society has taken advantage of them. I didn't feel that way.

17:10 (Beckett) On the opposite end, were there any places that you were like drawn away from, that you stayed away from when you came here to Danville?

17:18 (Ennis) Yes, yes, yes. This place called the Ponderosa, and I don't drink. I've never drank. I went to the Ponderosa once, and I told myself, then I would never go back, not because it was a bad place. It's just that that's not me, and that was, I guess, a place where we can, you know, that was a social gathering for most people.

17:53 (Beckett) So you mentioned you went and visited. You know, you're at the church a lot. You were obviously at your job site a lot. Were there other places in Danville that you've frequently visited every week or every day.

18:06 (Ennis) The gymnasium, I work out practically every day, so either coaching basketball or playing basketball or working out, so I'm like three places: the gymnasium, at my church, at home.

18:27 (Beckett) Okay so, what type of neighborhood did you live in when you moved here? You know, was it a like black neighborhood, a white neighborhood? Was it a good, a bad neighborhood? And could you describe, you know, how like–what your neighbors were like?

18:44 (Ennis) Well, when I first come to Danville, there was very few places to rent. You know, you want to live a place before you decide to buy a home. So I used to live out in the main apartment, which I think is called Berkley home now, but it was the enemy's apartment, and I wasn't looking for a particular place to live, I was looking for a nice place to live. Like I said before to me, we are the same. I don't look at as a matter of fact, I hate the word black and white. It it's not in my vocabulary, I want to be treated like I treat people and I treat them with kindness and love so but I lived in in the main for like, a year and a half, and looking through the neighborhoods, of course you dealing with realtors, and one thing I did notice about the realtors I was dealing with. We got some houses we want you to go take a look at, guess where they take me? To the black neighborhood, which was turning out for me. I can afford to live anywhere I want to live. This is not what I want. So we canned that realtor, and we end up getting some other realtors.

20:11 (Jacob) You mentioned how it was when you were growing up in Louisiana that, you know, tight knit community. You obviously raised your daughters here. Did you feel like you had that same community here in Danville?

20:23 (Ennis) Yes, I did, but guys and girls are different. You know, I can easily make friends, become socially involved, but for females, it's a little more challenging. But as far as raising my kids here, I don't think I could have chose a better place.

20:45 (Beckett) In your time in Danville, do you have any favorite story or stories from your time here? And would you like to share those with us today?

20:57 (Ennis) Yes, I think one of the favorite stories that I could share is the camaraderie that our church has had then. We had a softball team, and of course, to save money in painting our church, we as a bunch of guys playing softball. We swear, we get together and we're going to paint that church. So our softball team, we couldn't really paint, but we learned how to paint real fast, so we internally, we basically painted our whole church in two days. Our church softball team guys never been up on a ladder. Never have a paint brush in their hands, supposedly, but we got it done.

21:46 (Zeke) I'd like to backtrack a little bit and talk about some of your schooling experience when you were younger. So growing up for you and your siblings, going to like elementary, middle and high school was that like an integrated experience?

22:01 (Ennis) Well, our school was not integrated until 1968 so I was there for one year.

22:08 (Zeke) Okay, and obviously, I guess that's like a limited experience, but what were kind of the differences that you noticed, and how did that those experiences like shape you in your formative years?

22:21 (Ennis) Well, actually, I never noticed any of that, because I didn't grow up in that situation, but I had four Caucasian instructors, and one of them was a chemistry teacher, one was an English teacher, and one was a math teacher. But I enjoyed those teachers just as much as I did the rest of them. As a matter of fact, I like challenging teachers also. So to me, the work was pretty easy even though I studied.

23:04 (Zeke) And then you mentioned that you went to Southern University, that's an HBCU, correct? (Ennis) Yes, it is. (Zeke) And what was like the–you talk about the differences in experiences that you had, was there anything eye opening about your experience in HBCU that you didn't get in, like your high school or middle school experience?

23:26 (Ennis) What I had said earlier that the guy who had mentored me, he had prepared me to go to Southern basically to be an engineer. It's like late hours. You got to take classes in sequential order, and if you would have missed– well not missed, but if you would have missed a class that was offered the semester, you had to wait a whole year to get back. So I made sure that I didn't do that, and he had already informed me not to do that, because it's going to take you at least another year to get out of school. So really, I had an engineering degree. I only went to school three and a half years, but I went to school year round, just so I could take the classes in the sequential order so I could get out timely.

24:10 (Zeke) Yeah, and then you mentioned having a mentor, if you'd like to maybe share a little about who that was and what kind of impact they had on your life. Well, I forget the young man's name, but he was an engineer from Shell Oil Company, like I said earlier, from Baton Rouge, Louisiana, and he was a math guru, because I was taking algebra at the time, it was me and three other guys. We were pretty much probably on the same level, and he knew that we was taking vocational ed at the time. He noticed that we could do mechanical drawing, so he kind of took us under his wing and said, ‘Hey, you guys can compete at state level doing mechanical drawing and making furniture.’ So we did that for about two years, the four of us, and we got recognized for our talents, and that's just and then I just blossomed after that.

25:13 (Zeke) In your experience having a mentor, do you think that inspired you to be a mentor for other young men in your later years, and what kind of like values and experiences did you have being mentored that help you mentor other young men?

25:28 (Ennis) Yes, like I said earlier, it's my responsibility to give back, reach back and pull up. If I don't do that, I feel as though I'm cheating myself, as well as the community, and at one time, middle school had a mentoring program, and I mentored kids for about five and a half years.

25:52 (Zeke) So you mentioned that you have, like, a lot of brothers and sisters. Was there any type of, like, familial mentorship that was going on, either from, like, older, younger siblings?

26:01 (Ennis) Yes, my three older brothers, again, we used to challenge each other at everything we did, it was like, I can do something better than you. I'm the tallest of the guys in my family. And I had one brother, which was my third oldest brother. He always thought that he was the tallest. So every time we would get pretty close by, it was like, ‘I'm taller than you.’ This actually was growing up, but he never did get there.

26:31 (Zeke) And I think now we're going to jump in to talk a little bit about, like, urban renewal in Danville, if that's that's all right with you. So I understand that you moved to Danville in 1976 right? (Ennis) Yes. (Zeke) Which was a little bit after the completion of some of those urban renewal projects. So was that was urban renewal like a common topic of conversation when you moved here, and like, what was kind of the general consensus, and like, the feelings and sentiment of the people in Danville towards urban renewal?

27:01 (Ennis) I don't think I've ever been around a conversation wherever renewal was actually mentioned when I come here. One thing I did notice about Danville when I first come here, on Sundays, there was a place called used to go play basketball, which was Batewood homes, and just go play softball or baseball on Duncan Hill just every Sunday. But the word that people were using, we're going to go to the project and play basketball. And I'm saying, ‘Where's the projects?’ They said ‘Batewood Homes is a project.’ That's not a project. Where I'm from, a project is a project where there's no windows, no doors and clothes hanging out, but Batewood is a nice government subsidized community. I wouldn't consider that a project.

27:56 (Zeke) As someone who wasn't like necessarily immersed in Danville community at the time of the urban renewal projects, but you were there, like, in their immediate aftermath. Were there any type of feelings that you felt the community had towards the new buildings that were in place and like, in like, specifically the destruction of the Black Business District on Second Street? Like, how did Danville natives remember that?

28:22 (Ennis) Well, you're talking about today, you know, all the black businesses that Danville once had or owned, which was like I say, on Second Street, pretty much the whole block, even where our church is used to own that block of property. So our church is now landlocked, because, I guess we didn’t have no vision or fortitude to realize that, hey, you eventually got to have a place to park, those kind of things that the conversation is being held now.

28:50 (Zeke) And then, we spoke earlier with Melinda Weathers, who is a Danville native, and we asked her about if she's seen any types of initiatives taken to, kind of like, reconstruct some of the Black Business District? From your perspective, has there been any, any efforts to sort of reconstruct what was lost,

29:14 (Ennis) Not to my knowledge, not to my knowledge. I don't know of any at this point.

29:21 (Zeke) Alright, and then, kind of staying on the Second Street, was there any, like, lingering resentments that you felt within the community, in terms of that?

29:31 (Ennis) Not to my knowledge, like I say, I hang out by myself, basically.

29:42 (Jacob) I guess, from your perspective, having been in the community so much, Melinda Weathers talked about how there was a lack of a kind of drive to, like, get back into that ownership perspective that was lost when the urban rural project on Second Street was was enacted. Being like a mentor and being involved so heavily in church, was there ever any conversation about possibly being an entrepreneur and starting any businesses within Danville? Because when we talked to Melinda Weathers, she said that there, I don't think she could list a black owned business after that section of Danville was taken away. Was there was that ever a conversation that came up during your mentorship or during conversations within your church community?

30:34 (Ennis) Well, I've had probably two young men here recently say that they would like to be entrepreneurs, to own their own business. I said, “what type business would you like to own?” And one guy said, I would like to own a car dealership. And other guys said, Well, I want to be up on my own barber shop. And by them making those kind of comments, you know, you need to put things in front of them.
