Say how you get from point A to point A to point B, and I don't think it's just attached to what it used to be here on Second Street or downtown Danville, but that's what they want to do.

31:11 (Jacob) In terms of facilitating that, obviously, given that there are none, you would assume that it's pretty hard to start one here in Danville, given your involvement in the community, would you, do you think that there are certain aspects that you think Danville should help young black entrepreneurs to, you know, fulfill that dream, and if so, would you mind telling us a couple of them.

31:36 (Ennis) I think if anyone has the desire to become an entrepreneur, they need to be given the opportunities, and I guess, guide it in a direction which that, you know to get that support. Doesn't have to be black, but this is I don't like say. I don't like using those terms, even though I am an African American, but I don't

32:00 to me, I don't I don’t like the black and white thing.

32:05 (Jacob) We we've recently started looking at the great migration of African American people, leaving the south and going north in search of opportunities. You moved to Danville in 1976 and obviously became pretty easily involved within the community. Did you ever see like an exodus of your community around you, like searching for other opportunities outside of Danville?

32:36 (Ennis) Not really, you know, I can give you my experience. The guy I told you that mentored me from Shell Oil Company in Baton Rouge, Louisiana. I graduated from Southern likewise, I applied for jobs in Baton Rouge, Louisiana, but I never got an interview, an offer, so that, like I said, the ones I got was really up north. So even in 1973 there was still some issues with hiring minorities. But now, about four or five months after I was up north, I could have gotten a job with Shell Oil Company, but I chose not to.

33:24 (Zeke) So going back to sort of urban renewal. Were there any other experiences you had with that? Maybe in your hometown, in Baton Rouge.

33:33 (Ennis) Well my hometown was for Talula,

33:38 Yes, it wasn’t urban renewal. I can tell you, when they integrated the schools in my hometown, there was a school called Tallulah High which was, I'm sorry, where most of the Caucasian went, and then McCall High School, which was predominant school where I went

34:01 the week after,the state issued this command say, hey, we will integrate on this particular day, within like two weeks, those individuals that didn't want to go to school but African American. They had created another school in our little town called Richmond Billy, which was right across down there, they got to buy you same parish, but, but then, like half a mile that was a new school within two weeks.

34:31 This is where I'm from, in Talula.

34:36 (Zeke) And how do you think that like affected affected your community.

34:43 (Ennis) I think it affected drastically, because now you got to fund three schools as opposed to two.

34:49 (Zeke) So do you feel like the resources maybe were more spread? Spreads in between

34:53 (Zeke and Ennis) schools? Yes.

34:59 (Jacob) Is there anything else. You'd like to share with us about your time in Danville that kind of stuck out.

35:04 (Ennis) Yeah, I've enjoyed my time in Danville. Danville is a nice, quaint place to to live, raise a family, get involved.

35:17 I lived in Dallas, Texas for about maybe 13 months, didn't like it at all, just too busy. All you heard was sirens, fires, policemen, or whatever it was, just all day long.

35:36 (Zeke) So what are the positive things about Danville that you think made it a good place for you and your family to be?

35:44 (Ennis) Well, here in Danville, you can live where you can afford to live, and there is you can get involved as much as you want to get involved. I didn't say this either, but I was on the Chamber of Commerce for like 13 years. I was a member of the Danville Economic Development Partnership for six and a half years. I still am a member of this Workforce Development Committee, which is part of the Danville Boyle County Economic Partnership. So if you want to get involved, you can get involved. You can make a difference in the community.

36:26 (Zeke) Can you just quickly touch on your experience and kind of what things you were working on as part of the Chamber of Commerce and Economic Development here in Danville?

36:36 (Ennis) Okay, the workforce development, that the main purpose for workforce development is to increase the talent pipeline. And basically what we the original intent was to get more involved at a, say, a middle school level, expose students to all the opportunities that's available for employ, for employment, like I said earlier, everybody's don't want to go to college, but there are other avenues that you can pursue to become independent. And so that was part of the responsibility for workforce development.

37:19 (Zeke) And have you you kind of talk on the progress that you've made in that area and how that's affected, like you said, like the talent pool here in Danville.

37:30 (Ennis) It’s kind of hard for me to say, because I don't have the numbers on what has been done, but I do know for a fact that BCTC has increased the number of, I guess advanced manufacturing opportunities to educate students. I think they doubled it in probably the last four or five years.

37:49 (Zeke) And is that having, like, the positive impact immediately on Danville, or is it more like people are getting that experience than going elsewhere to utilize those skills?

37:57 (Ennis) I think both have probably immediate impact on Danville, but I don't know who's being employed by who, but I do know one of my previous Sunday school students. He is now a certified welder, and he obtained his certification through VCTC. Now where he's working, I don't really know, but I do know he's a certified welder.

38:19 (Jacob) Would you mind telling us a little bit about your experience with the Chamber of Commerce here in Danville, and what you guys kind of did with that?

38:31 (Ennis) It's trying to basically get out of the employers involved in supporting the commercial in other businesses here in Danville, of course the Chamber of Commerce is like, what do you got to offer to the community? What the community got to offer to you? And without that chamber, you wouldn't expose some things that's actually coming from the state level to these businesses. So that's basically what it is.

39:02 (Drew) I have kind of a question that's off topic. It's it's obvious that it's easy for you to integrate into whatever community you're a part of, but I'd say that's not the case for everyone, or even most people. So what? What advice would you give to someone who maybe struggles to integrate into the community?

39:27 (Ennis) One Be yourself and you treat people like you want to be treated. It's forget about the color of our skin, our background. We are all the same. When it comes down, we all the same. I think.

39:44 (Jacob) I do have a question. Obviously, throughout this interview, you've reiterated how important mentorship has been to you, and you've been doing that for a number of years. Was there ever an individual or like, a story around an. Individuals that really impacted you during your time at mentorship. And if you do have an individual, would you mind sharing a little bit about your journey with them?

40:11 (Ennis) I could tell you a student that really impressed me, that I mentored at Bate Middle School. This kid was in the eighth grade, and he was already taking high school courses. He was taking trigonometry at Denver high school as an eighth grader, and he was computer guru geek. He built his own computers, and of course, we also had job shattering they went along with the mentoring program. So when I was working at Whirlpool, what we call student exchange, where the students would actually go to the employees, and we had teachers exchange, where teachers would come. So the one student exchange, he wanted to be an electrical engineer, so I paired him up with an electrical engineer, and this young man basically could do the same work that engineers could do, because he was computing. So he went out to the University of Kentucky and ended up being an electrical engineer. And not only that, he was the only one that dressed the part. You know, he hit his sweatshirt on black tie, black slacks. He even pressed the management you know, he looked like he's been there.

41:41 (Drew) Again, off topic, but it just this is really interesting to me that you've competed against and with such high level athletes like Pistol Pete and, I believe Robert Parish. So, how, how have you sort of navigated being around such skilled individuals, and how has that affected you throughout your life?

42:08 (Ennis) Well, really, I guess it improved my life. You know, because being an athlete, you got to manage your time, and you got to always realize that you got to keep your skills as apex, you gotta be better than the next guy to stay on the court, and life is the same way. I pride myself in being different. I don't watch a lot of TV. I read a lot of technical books. So why most guys at home? As a matter of fact, when I was working prior to my retirement, practically every product that was designed I had to sign out finally before it actually went to the next step. I'm not bragging, that's just the way. And I got 14 patents done my work. There's no other questions. There any other stories or anything else you'd you'd like to discuss (Ennis) not really, but I'm glad that I got to participate in this interview. (Zeke) Do we have any questions from the group? Anyone that wants to ask right now? (Jeff Shenton) First, want to ask you quickly about the future someone who works with young people at First Baptist Church and other places. What do you think about the future of the church and the future of Danville? What do you see it in 15 or 20 years? What's what is Danville going to be like for the for the community? (Ennis) I would say Danville is going to be enhanced in 15 to 20 years, if, in fact, we allow young students to become part of the community, begin to listen to the young students.

44:13 As an example, my older daughter passed from lung cancer in 2013, and her daughter was 10 years old at the time, and of course, we made a promise to our daughter that we were raised her daughter, her husband, is a professional football player. Was a professional football player. Lavon Kirkland played with the Pittsburgh Steeler for nine years, and by playing professional football, there's no way you can raise a young lady. So she was 10 years old. So my wife and I, we tried, originally, to raise our granddaughter the same way we raised our daughters and. But it it wasn't good. Our granddaughter was more receptive to being asked to do things, as opposed to and listening to her as opposed to being told what to do. You know, you raising your kids wouldn't be perfect, but the grandkids are different. You get you treated with love, kindness and but our granddaughter, she's a junior now at NKU. She's a fine arts and theater major, and but she just got to listen to the younger generation. They are been exposed to a lot more things than we were at the same age. They are basically smarter than we were. They are really young adults, as opposed to young teenagers at the same age. (Jeff Shenton) Thank you for sharing that with us. With your granddaughter, for example, do you see her moving back to Danville, or where do you think she would would end up? What's, what's her desire for the future? Do you think her desire to be in New York, on the big stage? (Ennis) As a matter of fact, she's in a play. She's the what? She's a wiki witch in the Wizard of Oz. The play airs the 31st of January in Cincinnati. As a matter of fact, this is a first paid gig. Yes, she says she's gonna become employed. She's getting paid to be on the stage. That's okay. You got to pay taxes. Now I see her being like, say, New York big city, because that's her desire, and she takes it serious.

46:52 (Ennis) I tried to pay her to play basketball. She's six feet tall. Her mom played basketball. And I told us, well, she played volleyball for two years and played basketball for two years, and her dad's an athlete. Her mom's an athlete, and my other two grandkids are athletes, one place for the Houston Texan, the other mid grand she runs track for the University of Louisville. She has a master's degree, both and got master's degrees. I said, Well, just go practice with the basketball team for just practice this fall and play one game. I give you $2,500 she said, ‘Let me think about it, Papa. Nope, I'm not gonna’ She went through so from that point on, we never mentioned anything that's about being an athlete, six feet tall, long arm shop out of the gym. But her heart's not in it. So she wants to be an actress. So she's pursuing her dreams.

48:14 (Audience) How long did you do the Mentor Program at Bate Middle School?

48:18 (Ennis) What they did was like five years. But they don't have it now, but the time I was doing it was five years. As a matter of fact, Ann Yanger was the director of it. (Audience) So I find it so interesting and so cool that you grew up with 12 other siblings. Kind of want to know where did they end up, how did their story differ from you? Since you all had like, an upbringing together, I think quite here, but you say, okay, so I found it very interesting that you had 12 siblings. Amazing. I love big families. I want to kind of know how where your siblings ended up. How did their lives differ from yours? Maybe, since you guys all had an upbringing life together, okay,

49:18 (Ennis) I got eight living siblings now. My oldest brother, he served in the army. He retired from the army. He's now deceased. My second oldest brother, he was an electrical engineer, and he owned his own business as an electrical contractor. Likewise, he's now deceased. He's been deceased eight years. And my third brother, he was a professional painter. And the brother that's next to me, he is a maintenance engineer at a hospital in Delhi, losing. Anna,

50:01 My older sister, which was next to him, she is now deceased, and my next brother. He works for the he's a maintenance, no, he's basically a supervisor at the AT&T stadium where the Cowboys play. And my next brother, he's retired because he used to work for offshore oil drilling and my next sister, she's married to a minister, so she's, she doesn't do anything. And my next sister, she's, she runs a after school, nursing program I hit they hit start that she lives in uh, Missouri. Obviously they were a big part of your childhood. Did as you guys grew up and moved away, did you guys remain in touch, or were they a big part of, like, your life after college? Yes. As a matter of fact, I was just down there for Christmas. It's nothing like family. It's nothing like family. And as a matter of fact, it's, it's three or four of them could be comedians. Yeah, yeah. They have you chuckling. Man. We talk about how we grew up, what we used to do, stuff you had no clue that they did, but and then our mom would say, Well, I knew what was going on, what she really did.

52:02 (Jacob) So you mentioned that you lived kind of all over the US. You obviously grew up in Louisiana. You now live in Danville. You mentioned Texas at one point. Have you noticed any like did you have any like culture shocks, or any like major differences in the like community between the cities that you lived in? (Ennis) No, I have not only because the first thing I did when I went to a community, I would find a place to play basketball, whether it was a recreation center outdoors. I always met guys, but my wife, she may tell you something different. I remember going to Schenectady, New York. I got in the park and started playing basketball, you know, to play basketball. So the first thing I would do to let everybody know I could jump a little bit. I would get a basketball donkey and donkey, you know, and I had no trouble. Somebody picking me up. Hey, we want to go up next. You want to come to Danville? It was the same way. Get out of the car, get warm up, go dunk a couple of times. Hey, you want to go up next. I never had a problem.

53:17 (Jacob) Would you mind telling us a little bit about your wife and whether she had the similar experience here in Danville, (Ennis) I think was a little more difficult for my wife to actually get involved in the community, only because I was probably more outgoing than she was. But my wife retired from East McDowell after 35 years, she was the director of the volunteer services. She has a degree in Human Resource Management, but she's still supervising. You know, she's retired, but she's supervising. She has a bucket list that, for some reason, I get one out of the bucket. She'll put two or three in. So it's never in it she's still supervising. And we've been married for like, 53, years. (Jeff Shenton) Where did you meet her?

54:23 (Ennis) In grade school. This is my childhood sweetheart. We started dating, she was 13, I was 15 and I got a brother that married her younger sister, all in the family, and the brother that was older than me, but they did her older sister, but for some reason, that didn't work out. And my mother in law and my mom were best friends. (Jeff Shenton) last questions or anything to add. Alright, thank you so much, Mister Chair. (Ennis) Thank you. If you ladies and gentlemen, feel free to come to First Baptist.
horaceross:
Horace Ross

“born and raised there in Danville, Kentucky. Born June 17, 1944, educated in the Boyle County School Systems, Bate school.”

Transcript

00:03 All right, morning, afternoon. My name is Ryan Hall, and today I'm interviewing Reverend Horace Ross, who is a Danville native and a musician here. Where'd it go? Oh, I'm here with Owen Techet, Jasper Staley, and today is January, 23 2025. We're recording this interview in African American Historical Society in Danville, and today we'll be discussing Reverend Ross's experiences during urban renewal.

51:38 Yes, sir, mainly, but, I mean, we kind of just wanted to know how it was like growing up during that time, and kind of what you got into in terms of job wise, and how you know during that time, how, or if any sort of kind of segregation was involved in your life. And that's kind of what we've been wanting to know a little bit.

52:07 Yeah, we've talked quite a bit in the class about there were several funded urban renewal projects in Denville. And what we're interested in is the way in which people experience that. And so it's interesting to us as much about your experience on old wilderness, and about how there really wasn't much in the way of urban renewal down there as well, and the fact that you left early and then came back after a lot of those projects that happened. So it's really good context for us to understand how people perceive those those projects. But we have talked about that quite a bit in the class

52:40 I see. Okay,

52:44 so you said your your father's restaurant was really just closed because it was, it wasn't profitable anymore. Do you know what year that was? Do you know about what, what year that that closed?

52:59 Just guessing. I'm thinking probably the middle 60s, 65, 66 somewhere in there.

53:09 And was that the same with other businesses on Second Street too? Were they closing in the mid 60s? Were they less profitable and there were less less folks visiting Second Street by then?

53:20 Yeah. I'm not sure of the rest behind what happened, how they got forced out, or

53:34 I'm not certain, because looks, I wasn't, I wasn't there, but, I don't know.

53:49 So we're coming up on about an hour. Is there anything that you wanted to talk about or didn't get a chance to mention that you wanted to talk

54:00 about? No sir. So I don't think so. No, everything. Think everything's been covered.

54:11 I'm curious a little bit about your thoughts on on your your family and in the future. You said you have two grandchildren. Is that right? Yes. What do you think about their their lives and their prospects in the world? What do you think they'll be doing in in 20 years? Do you think?

54:30 Well, I'm thinking my granddaughter will probably be some sort of business owner. She's very talented, very creative. She makes jewelry , she's excellent painter. I think she'll probably go into the arts of some sort. Grandson, I haven't put a finger on him yet. He I'm not sure about him yet. I.

55:00 Fair enough. Fair enough. Would you say that you know the prospects of you know what families, African American families from Danville, what they have in front of them and what they can do is changed over the course of your lifetime. How would you how would you describe that change,

55:19 the change that's happened? Yeah,

55:26 I'm looking at looking at it two ways. Some folk that stayed in Danville, they didn't leave, and they stayed and they took, I guess,

55:46 I don't know. It didn't seem like they really, I don't know. I'm not sure. I think that they I think that the folks that left

56:02 got exposed to more, if you know what I'm saying, they got exposed to more, and ones that stayed, I don't know. It seems like they fell into a state and they stayed there. I mean, it didn't, of course, I know devil got a black mayor. Now, first one, and it took a long, long time to get there.

56:31 They had a black chief of police, but it's taken so long for that to happen. And so it seems like for folks who have stayed there to actually Elevate, it seems like it's it was kind of stagnant,

56:57 while folks that left it seems like they've done better. I know, looking at some of my classmates and so forth, doctors, lawyers, Indian chiefs. I mean, they're, I said, Indian chief, but one was a fire fire chief,

57:19 paramedic fire chief, and I don't know, it seems like, seems like they've done better when they moved away and came back.

57:32 Thanks for sharing that perspective with any other questions.

57:36 I just had one more. So I'm from Memphis, and you know, we've grown up, I learned a lot about can the music industry Memphis, and especially about BB King.

57:49 Your mic sounds kind of fuzzy. I have trouble hearing that one a little bit

57:56 I was going to ask. So I'm from Memphis, and growing up, I learned a lot about music and history music down in Memphis, and I was just wondering if there was anyone that you grew up listening to, like maybe BB King or Ray Charles that kind of influenced you in your music career.

58:17 No

58:20 Norman Smith, even though he wasn't a musician, he got into the music world. He was a publisher, and I think he was actually recognized down there in Atlanta for his work in the music industry. But as far as anybody else, I'm not, I don't think that there's anybody else that I know of that was successful in the music business. Well, there's a younger, younger man. Name is Lionel Harris. He went into the music field, but he he's on the religious, religious side. Michael, do you have anything on him? I

59:26 did you ask him about Michael? He was, yeah, he's, he's not on the call. Oh, he's not on the call. No, sir.

59:33 Oh, okay, I thought Michael was there. But anyway, yeah, there was a fella by the name of Ronnie o Harris, who done great in the music industry? Thanks,

59:46 yeah, we'll have to ask him about that. Just say, yeah, he might, he might have some information on that. We'll ask him about that. Thank you,

59:51 yeah. Okay, all right. Well,

59:56 we thank you so much for your time. We really appreciate the. Fact that you took the time to do this, and we will certainly share this with you once it gets archived and posted. Okay, thank you very much. Thank you so much. Have a great day. Thank you. Applause.
jameshunnmichaelhughes:
James Hunn & Michael Hughes

“I started at an early age, about eight or nine years old.”

Transcript

00:00:00 Zamarripa: My name is Cindy Zamarripa, and today, I am interviewing James Hunn and Michael Hughes, who worked in agriculture in central Kentucky. I am here with Jeffery Shenton, and today is May 26, 2023. We are recording this interview in the Danville Boyle County Historical Society (Danville Boyle County African American Historical Society), and today we will be discussing agriculture and farmwork and their experience in the 20th century.

00:00:34 Zamarripa: Hi

Hughes: Hi.

Hunn: Hey.

Zamarripa: All right, how are you guys doing?

Hughes and Hunn: Good.

Zamarripa: Awesome; So, what kind of farmwork did you guys do or agriculture work, and when did you guys start?

00:00:51 Hunn: I started at an early age, about eight or nine years old. My father started out when I first realized about farming or working, you know. My father was a sharecropper, and I did not know what kind of contract he had because I was still too young. I was about four or five years old. But later, around 1940’s, 45’s, 44’s, 44-45.

Hunn: He was living on a farm; we were [was] living on a farm. With two other families, black families on that farm, that was working on the farm.

00:01:43 Zamarripa: Did you guys split the farm? Or did you separate for your own individual stock, garden, or communal?

Hunn: We had individual gardens, but I don’t know what the farmer’s owner provided. But later on, I do know what my father raised. In the late 44’s and late 45’s or early 46’s. My father moved back to our old place when he had his own farming, well, family farm. Then we started, well, he started raising his own crops. He was raised me his own garden of hogs, cows, tobacco, and corn.

Hughes: A little garden.

Hunn: The only thing we had to buy was sugar and mostly coffee. He had grains then, corn for the mill and wheat for flour.

00:02:51 Zamarripa: Was the mill on the farm, or was it somewhere else?

Hunn: He always bought it. The flour, the wheat to make our flour to ground, grow our own corn and our garden food. They did a lot of canning and preserving. We always had a cow to kill for beef, and hogs to eat for pork meat and raise chickens. Everyone raised chickens. So, that was early, we are beginning tobacco season, they had to burn tobacco beds in order to sow the seeds. Then you had to weed it, then cover it with plastic at first. After plants start coming up, they would put canvas over the top of the tobacco bed. It could be anywhere from of 50 to 150 feet long. Then you had to weed the tobacco bed until the plants got up. While this was going on, the ground was beginning to prepare to plant the plants* [seedlings]. Then, although someone would have to hoe it, what was planted it. There was (two) couple of ways to plant it. They would plant it sometime in the center, two men center, and sometimes somebody would have a one-man center. Which, you would drop the plant down to the center, but a two-man center, it was pulled by horses, two horses. Either, years later on up in the 50’s, late 50’s or early 60’s it was done by a tractor. Two men, they would have trays, on each side, and they would reach for plants and drop down in between them. Then water, fertilize it and everything, at the same time.

Hughes: Then, after they grown to become so firm, the tobacco was topped, it led them to mature, and get ripe. Then they were [was] starting to cut it, and you had to go, it would grow, it was called suckers, and suckers would grow on the plant. 0:05:10

Zamarripa: So, what are suckers?

Hunn: It was like more plants coming up on the top of it, and they would bloom, the blooming part of it. In between the leaves of it, there would be little up shoots of them too. So they all had to be cut out, before they could cut up the tobacco. So then, after all the suckers were removed, they would start cutting it, they would take and drop the sticks that were ours and then you start cutting it. Sticks were dropped in between two rows, while you cut from each side.

00:05:47 Zamarripa: Like sticks of tobacco?

Hunn: Yeah, tobacco sticks. We had anywhere from six to eight stocks of tobacco sticks. Then, sometimes, we would let them lay for two or three days before housing them in the barn. Then we house them in the barn and on the rails. Sometimes, later on in the fall, they would start stripping it and grading it. There was trash, lugs, reds, and tips.

Hugh: Brights.

Hunn: Yeah, brights or tips. Reds and brights are about the same thing.

00:06:23 Zamarripa: Is that based on corn or a separate thing?.

Hunn: Well, you had a table, [ demonstrates with Mr. Hunn hands an elongated table] about this long, or a little bit longer. You had trash, lugs, brights, then tips.

Zamarripa: Reds and Tips? Reds or brights are tips. Tips were the very—if you were starting at the very top, or if we were starting with trash— the lowest part next to the ground, that was trash because it was ragged and everything. The lugs was the very best of the tobacco, and the reds were the second part too them. The tips and the red were really bad, and gummy too.

00:07:21 Zamarripa: So, they didn’t taste very good?

Hunn: And when they dried, they had to wait until the rain came, when the water came so they could Strip it. So, it wouldn’t tear it all up; if it was dry it could crumble all up, so when it dried, it would be too crumbly. So, when it rained, it would get all soft and would shape it. Then they tie them into hands, then [inaudible]. I always used grease from the lugs to cover my hands because it lasted longer and was much better and less sticky, better together when on the stick. Then you would put it in books. What you would call a book. It’s when somebody sticks one way (one direction) and the other way (opposite direction) and then across of it, sort like a checkerboard.

00:08:20 Zamarripa: So, you would lay it in order-

Hunn: Until it gets so high. Then it was ready to take to the market. When they took it to the market, they had big bags and would pull the sticks out and lay them on one those baskets. Then it was ready for sale.

Hunn: For the corn, we were [was] probably too big to help, what you call women weeders. It was sowed in the winter months within the fall of the year, all the crops were. When harvest come up, it would be ready to be harvested in the spring. Some men would just come by the field and just clean all the seeds out. The corn was usually harvested in the fall too, so when it got dry, a lot of time, they would cut it. For some, they would stack it in shucks. They would plant it, kind of a checkboard pattern, so they wouldn’t have to go very far to make a big shuck. Most time when there was snow on the ground, you got out there to shake to shake it off.

Hughes: A lot of it was done by night too, by the moonlight.

00:09:50 Zamarripa: Was it easier?

Hunn: I knew I did it back then—

Hughes: I used to go out with my dad and help him shuck corn-

Hunn: Because he worked during the daytime.

00:10:00 Zamarripa: Did you guys have skin problems with the sun or dehydration issues?

Hunn: As you can tell, our skin is a little darker—

Zamarripa: [Laughter of Embarrassment]

Hunn: Other than that- well, some people sunburn too, you know? But I never did.

Zamarripa: I was thinking about the long hours too; besides the darkening of your skin, you can get really sick, like sun sickness.

Hunn: Noo, Noo. I never did. But we were all good. Chicken as well. Chicken too were harvested during the summertime too. My dad would always order them fifty baby chickens every spring. And most of the time, when he finally got a freezer, he would freeze a bunch of them. You know we have to kill them and pluck them. Then we would cut them up and freeze them. For a lot of vegetables, they would bury them. Cabbage and potatoes. They would take straw—they would start with a hole and put straw in it, and they would put potatoes and carrots in there, and they do it kind of a layer on it. Then a layer of cabbage and potatoes on one side. One side of the stack maybe all potatoes, while another might be cabbages on one side. And they would cover it all with straw. And then would put hay on top of it.

00:11:32 Zamarripa: Was this some kind of decomposer or compost?

Hunn: No, you would just cover them and then put dirt over it. During the wintertime, if you wanted cabbage or potatoes, you go out there right in the back get you some-

Zamarripa: Oh!

Hunn: It was a keeping process. Sometimes, they would put carrots in them too and beets in that stack too. So, you always had fresh vegetables during the wintertime.

00:12:06 Zamarripa: So, it kept persevering them mostly?

Hunn: Yeah, it would persevere them. Then you would eat them. A lot of it was canned too. Beans, Green beans, what else? Most of these were canned tomatoes, tomato soup, green beans, apples, and any kind of fruit. And peaches, all were preserved in jars.

Shenton: And who would do that work? The preservation works?

Hughes: It was the women, no doubt.

Hunn: Mostly the women, they would wash them. I remember there being three tubs, water, washboard, and then you would call brewing it in rich [inaudible]  To rinse. Some of boards the were made from glass or plastic so that you could rub them.

00:13:07 Zamarripa: Did you help out as a kid? Favorite type of jam or anything?

Hunn: Oh yeah. Like we always had sausage.

Zamarripa: Yeah.

Hunn: We always helped around making sausage, made the lard, and all that. When they were growing [inaudible] hold them, keep them cleaned out so our [inaudible] weight takes all by itself. It is a big process. In the fall, usually about Thanksgiving time is when it was hog-killing time.

Zamarripa: Oh.

Hunn: Processing the hogs, yeah.

Zamarripa: It’s a long process, I am assuming?

Hunn: Yeah, it was. After, once you scald them, it was easy, you know? The hair comes off really easy there. Then they hung them up.

Hughes: Yeah

Hunn: Most of the time, you would shoot them in the head, then cut their throats while they hung them up. Let the blood drain out.

00:14:12 Zamarripa: Did you guys have a separate area for the draining of the blood?

Hunn: No, just-

Zamarripa: Just?

Hunn: You just drain where you’re at.

Zamarripa: So not in the kitchen.

Hunn: Draining was right next to the scalding of the hogs.

Zamarripa: Ah okay.

Hunn: Our house was at the end of the [****] road [ Inaudible] It was like ditch, you would put the scalding box right there while they fill up for the frying of it. The heat would burn your hair off and leaves nearby.

Shenton: Was this a community event, was it multiple families or just one?

Hunn: Yeah, multiple families most of the time it was. And sometimes it would be just one well, with three to four men to together to get to kill in a place together. And everybody, locally, would come together to one place to kill their hogs.

Zamarripa: Would people bring food as well?

00:15:13 Hunn: Most of the time they would cook pieces of the hog, while they were making the lard. Yeah, and then would cut a lot of the fat meat up into small pieces to make the lard then they had one of straights, there was an apparatus that would pressure in, and down all is what we called cracklings.

Zamarripa: OHH! Yes!

Hunn: Meat skin and cracklings.

Shenton: So, these were like all the families that worked, and were sharecroppers on a particular farm that would come together? Or is a larger community than that?

Hunn: Where I was brought up, the community at that time was forty houses with forty families living in the community. Well today’s there’s almost no one on it, they live two blocks away from that area now. I have two nephews, well only one nephew that lived there and he just headed out.

Shenton: So, would the family have to travel to the farm where you were doing the agriculture work? Or was it, that, local?

Hunn: Most of them back then, yes. When they, if most of them weren’t living on the farm, they would travel to the farm to which they were working at.

Shenton: So, like your father, how far would he have to travel to do all the work you were talking about, like the corn, and the wheat, and the tobacco.

Hunn: Well, right there at his home, he had his own farm, he was doing his own business.

Zamarripa: How big was it? Was it like one, two, three acres? Or?

Hunn: It was about ten to twelve acres.

Shenton: So, he was doing his own cash crops, as well with tobacco, corn and wheat on his own land-

Hunn: Yeah

Shenton: In addition to another place?

Hunn: No, he didn’t do any- after he moved back, he didn’t do any, he just worked on the farm for a daily wage or hourly wage.

Shenton: Then he would come home and do the same thing at home?

Hunn: No, we did it most of it ourselves

Shenton: So, you were doing it at home?

Zamarripa: Yeah

Hunn: When we were small, we got to feed the chickens, slop the hog, feed the dogs, take care of the cows, yeah. Pigs. A lot of the time, they had the distillery over by Camp Nelson they would go to get that whey, not whey, the byproducts of the whiskey making and bring it back to put more feed into it to make a liquid. They make the feed to fed the cows and hogs.

Shenton: Like the mash?

Hunn: Yeah! Like the mash, and that’s what you would feed the hogs. And beside water, then you had to build a hole for them for laying in the summertime. Because they don’t sweat. So, I had to keep them cool with water and mud.

Shenton: So, I want to make this hundred percent, just really crystal clear, you had the community in which you lived in—which is about forty houses and that’s where all the livestock was and the gardens, is that right?

Hunn: Everyone had their own place, yeah.

Shenton: But all the cash crops, the tobacco, wheat and the corn was all elsewhere on they were they were working for a wage, is that right?

Hughes: Yes sir.

Hunn: Well yeah, they worked in those places too.

Shenton: Yeah, but locally there was no cash crop, like your family didn’t bring tobacco to the market to sell?

Hunn: Yes, they did,

Shenton and Zamarripa: Yes? They did, okay.

Hunn: We grew tobacco, corn and wheat, but for wheat we had to get that somewhere else. You had to go one, two, three, store to buy a bushel.

Shenton: And tobacco and corn you sell at the market, is that right?

Hunn: Yeah, you get your feed for your hogs and everything.

Zamarripa: Were there specific farmer events you guys would go to sell? Or was more people knew you were selling, and they would come and ask for it?

Hunn: We just took it to the market, and that’s where they sold it at.

Zamarripa: Where were the markets located at?

Hunn: One of them was in Hustonville and Stanford. They call them farmer stores. And you could get other things. Most of the farmers that had, well, one farmer, he had in our neighborhood a grounder. He got ground it on his own feed.

00:19:58 Hunn: He had a truck—tractor—one he could hook it up to and he rode that machine with fresh corn in it. Many white farmers had sheep, cows, and horses that they raised.

Zamarripa: Was it more accessible for them to have sheep or horses versus you guys- were you guys able to? Was there a limitation as to what you could own?

Hunn: Well, in a while, the blacks did what they could for food— not everybody needs sheep. But there were other farmers’ beds, with cows, hogs, and sheep and a few horses.

00:20:50 Shenton: Michael, what was your experience with farming?

Zamarripa and Hun: [Overlapping laughter]

Hunn: I was about to say-

Hughes: Well, he pretty much described the whole, pretty much; it was the same way. Just about everything he said, this happened because a lot of the men would work on sharecropping on other people’s farms. My stepfather had a really small little farm. My grandfather, he pretty much had the whole thing. He raised tobacco, he raised corn, some corn. He didn’t raise much corn. His big thing was tobacco. For a garden, he had a garden was like, it looked it went from here to there[ gesturing to the place of the building, implying it was large], and he would wake us up when I stayed with him, he would get us up at six o’clock or six-thirty in the morning.

Zamarripa: Just before the roosters.

Hughes: Oh yeah, on a Saturday morning, he would have us picking beans, corn and grain. But you know I did the same thing he [Hun] talked about working in tobacco. I did all that. I used to hate having to weed the tobacco bed. Those were one of those things that you didn’t really want to do if you didn’t have too.  You learn how to ride the setter, what is called a setter.

Hughes: Me and Him [ shown as an example of how the settling process worked] I put one down, he puts one down as it went down the row. I would drop one and then he would drop one.

Shenton: But also, the shoots, the actual little plants?

Hughes: Yeah, you would have the actual plants on your lap.

Hunn: The machine made a row. It also had a shoot when you dropped it down, you would bring back to by hand in order to cover it up?

Hughes: It would open and you would have to go cover it back up after you dropped that plant.

Zamarripa: Assuming you’re talking about a plow? Or individual holes would be made?

Hughes: No.

Hunn: No.

Hunn: That’s another way [Laughter]

Zamarripa: I’m sorry. I was getting confused. [Laughter]

Hun: Now, one man center did that. When you dropped it [plants] in the one man center, the guy operating the one man center would take his feet and close it up—used his foot to close up the hole.

Hughes: Then, you would have to come back—Somebody would come back after the setting, to replant the ones that were bad. Its what you call replant.

Hunn: You would have to go back to cut down the plant, so you replant it.

Hunn: Then many people, with smaller places, had tobacco patches, did it by hand. They had a peg. It was like a wooden peg. Usually, the handle was slightly curved over, which is a like a cane, you know how those cane had them curve handle on them and it was straight out and it would be sharp pointed. You would stick it down to the ground and wiggle it a little to make the hole. Then you would stick the plant in it then cover it up.

Zamarripa: Oh! Okay.

Hunn: But it had to be wet; when you replant when you reset it.

Hughes: Yeah, when you reset it.

Hunn: Yeah, it had to be wet, so after rain or something. For the plant to start growing there.

Hughes: Because you would know, after somebody went through them on the setter. You could see what plants were growing and which plants you had to reset.

Hunn: Yeah.

Hughes: Then you had to come back, and chop weeds up and around the whole thing. Then, like he said, you come as a group, you know, you had to chop weeds out. After it got to a certain height, suckers would grow on them. Then you had to come back to top. They called it topping.

Hunn: And sometimes, they do something called a side dressing. They put a little bit of fertilizer down the side of each row. Running down each row, in order to help it grow. Most time it was combination of ash and [inaudible]

00:25:15 Zamarripa: Did the pile of ash come from the kitchen fire, or where did the ash come from?

Hunn: No, it turns out they had places that manufactured fertilizer. They used to have one out here over by- is that plant still over by [inaudible]

Hughes: Which one?

Hunn: The old fertilizer plant over by the [inaudible]

Hughes: Yeah, I think so. But what was the name—I can’t think of a name.

Hunn: Neither can I, it was something like, State, but we had a lot of them. We had a lot of fertilizer too. Then for the summer farming, the fertilizer was a combination; and that was two, three different types of tobacco and corn to help it set.

Hughes: One of things that we talk about raising chickens—is on a Sunday. Our house, I learned early if it was going to be Sunday dinner with fried chicken. That you had to go out and kill the chicken.

00:26:26 Zamarripa: Mhmm.

Hughes: And you had to chase it down, grab it, then snap its neck.

Zamarripa: So, you would just grab it and snap—I know their other ways to kill a chicken—Did you guys, like, use the bag trick? Did you guys ever do that one?

Hughes: Nah, I learned just to grab them then slam them. Their body would go one way and flopping—

Hunn: I liked to take my foot and stick their head and then just pull them up. A lot of the time, we used to hang them on the clothesline. We would like throat cut them and leave them hanging. There was two, three different ways. —A lot of times people would put a stick on their necks and pull it up too. There was several different ways to kill them.

00:27:18 Hughes and Huns: Overlapping audio

Hunn: When we were kids, my mother died early, so we, my father, when he was out working, we were, and he kept us all five of us together. When he was out working, we got up to what we were counted on, like looking after ourselves. And his sister stayed with us about a year or two after my mother died, to take care of us while my father worked. And what we would do, we would take what we weren’t using. If we wanted chicken, we would kill our chicken. This is what we would do. We would take a kernel of corn and run a needle through it and tie a string to it as a knot on that corn kernel, then throw it over the fence, and pull it. Then the chicken would swallow it, then we would pull that chicken back to us. [Laughter] We would walk over to the top of the fence and then take it home, then kill it. We would pick it and clean everything and we would burn the feathers so daddy wouldn’t see. Once in a while, he was a little mad.

Zamarripa: Did he ever find out?

Hughes: I tell you, there was a consequence, and that’s the word I am going to use. When I was small, I probably killed four or five chickens and just bring them in, breaking their necks by stepping on them, showed me by my dad or stepfather on how to do it. So, one day my mother told me to go to the hen house and get a couple of eggs. So I’m like, what I’m like nine, ten years old—nah I was probably like eight. And so I’m walking down towards the hen house, and you know, I’m singing since I’ve been there like sixteen times over there, I’m just walking and singing and then I noticed the old rooster—

Zamarripa: Yeah.

Hughes: Kinda walking with me, so you know, I go to the hen house, get the eggs. I started to turn towards the house; the old rooster started walking towards me and when I got the kitchen door of our house- HE JUMPED ON MY BACK and started flocking me, man.

Hunn: [Laughter]

Zamarripa: Oh so, he knew!!

Hughes: Oh yeah, he knew that I had killed some of them hens. And I would just be at the door, and my mom opened the door, I fell in the door, with me and the rooster both fell in the door.

00:29:52 Zamarripa: [Laughter]

Hughes: Then she got him off me. But I don’t know what that was something I—But it was one of those experiences I would never forget. I don’t think I killed more chickens after that. But I don’t remember but I do remember working in the tobacco. One of my favorite things to do was, when the time came, was cutting tobacco. That was the best time of the year because you made more money—Which when I first got started working on cutting the tobacco, I made a dollar and a quarter an hour for the same hard work. But when you are a kid and you worked all week, you would make about forty to fifty dollars—Everything that James [Jim] talked about pretty much went through the same scenario.

Hunn: When I started cutting them, I was making five dollars a day.

Hughes: I think I made about six dollars a day working in the hay.

Hunn: Somebody would pay us to stack straw as one of things. When I first got started, I was about nine and they would pay you fifty cents an hour.

Hughes: I loved hanging on the top rail that led straight towards the top.

Hunn: Helping to put up them sticks.

Hughes: Everyone would be helping to get down every stick- [overlapping audio] I have worked bottom rail-

Hunn: I know one of things that I did, when I was a kid, we had a bunch of hogs, and bunch of pigs, and one kept getting out all the time. So, I decided I was going to ring it.

00:31:56 Zamarripa: Oh?

Hunn: Yeah, they would put rings that go in the nose, keeps them from rooting the damn thing, and I decide that I was going to ring him one day. So, I had a friend, he would always come up to the neighbor’s house to get milk every day; and so, him and I, we ringed it out. And so, I got in too deep, and he was still a small pig, and he couldn’t nurse nor eat so he was, we were left with something.

Hunn: One day, my dad comes home and notices on the pig. He said, “Did you ring that?” I said, “Yes, I did.” He cut the ring out and whooped me; and then when the pig finally died, he whooped me again. [Laughter]

So, he would come in, he would go to certain neighborhoods, and you know, they would be waiting for him. Because like James [Jim] was saying, people grew their own stuff- and black folks would wait, rather than going to the store and buy the canned goods and stuff, you know. They would wait for him to come, and he would, he was kind of like rolling mobile store, I guess you can say, you know.

Hunn: My school, the school that I went to in the first room school that I went to, was the same town he lived there. Hughes: I think when we go back and talk about mostly black folks coming in from slavery, they start working on white farms. And I had one over here. He would help me and I would help him. Then we would go together, and you know, I would help him in his tobacco. We get his tobacco. Then he would come over, and help me get my tobacco, and same thing. For hay, or even killing hogs, and all of that. They all worked together, you know to, you know.

Shenton: So black families that own pieces of land is what you’re saying? They were working communally on each other and individually owned pieces of land. Is that right?

Hughes: Yeah.

Hunn: Yeah, My great-grandfather, on my father’s side, he started then buying property. He was a mulatto. Number one, he was a mulatto, and he started buying property in 1868’s right after the civil war, a small piece of property. Shenton: Is that all contiguous, is all linked together?

Hunn: No, all of them were different, some of them were miles apart. The first pieces he bought in 1868 were inside of a lot of the land. So, he had, to go through somebody else property, to get to it. Some of them were just lots of land.

But its actually a creek, and some of them were pretty deep too,  and some of them would be running off the side of the hill, you know. You would cut a road through it, to cut timber.

Hunn: Yeah, the last timber was cut off in 98’s or 99’s, almost the 2000’s I think.

Hunn: Nothing else. That’s all. Well, you let somebody hunt on it or something. But I never hunted on it.

Hughes: Yeah, 55’s before I got introduced, but it was the same thing. You know.

Hunn: Before I did, you were right, I did start in the 40’s.

Hughes: Yeah.

Hunn: Because I started high school in 54’.

Hughes: I think I was about ten or eleven an I actually got a wage, you know what I am saying.

Hunn: Doing tobacco, or even start cutting tobacco, when I was about twelve or thirteen years old. Then by the time we got to high school, we had to buy our own books.

Hughes: Clothes and books.

Hunn: Clothes and books and soap. My older brother, [****] and [****] he would always buy soap, I would help my sisters, to help them buy their schoolbooks and some clothes. So, Daddy didn’t have to do it, well he had to do it, but you know. At the same time, I bought extra meat too. And see, what I say, besides the bushels, we always bought sugar and coffee. Then we couldn’t drink the coffee, because we would drink it all. Because during this time, they would tell us that drinking coffee would make us black. [Laughter] Coffee will make you black, so you can’t drink coffee.

Zamarripa: [Laughter] That’s something to say.

Shenton: All that, the wage work all on land, like large pieces of land that were owned by white landowners?

Hughes and Hunn: Yeah, it was.

Hunn: Most of them but not all of them.

Hughes: Very few people, very few black people owned their own you know. Like my stepfather, he never owned but we had a little bitty garden within the property that he had but as far for tobacco places, and corn and stuff that was usually on somebody else land.

Hun: He had a place of his own, so his place [ inaudible] when he was living on the farm, my daddy’s brother lived on that farm, but he already owned but he wasn’t living there, he was living on the farm. When he moved, he made his own place he started sharecropping for another place. Then he raised tobacco, corn, hay. That was left out, a lot of hay was grown too. And a lot of corn was used to store. You’ve seen the big and tall, the silos, they used corn or sometimes hay in them too. Somebody had to be on one, two, three, men to get on the inside, and they would use young boys or men to store them.

Hughes: Yeah, I was one of them.

Hunn: I was one too. I can remember that, and you couldn’t get out until the next hole, or the next hole.

00:44:02 Zamarripa: Oh wait-

Hughes: They would bring them and don’t forget you had to get out of the way, or you had to crawl all the way down.

Hunn: Yeah, see every time you got up to another window, you couldn’t get out until you got the next one above you to get you out, or get to the very top.

Hughes: You sometimes had to be there for days before you even get the chance to get out.

Hunn: The way you got in, was the way you got out.

Zamarripa: Where were there any accidents during those times?

Hunn: Not that I know of.

Zamarripa: Okay.

Hughes: Sure, there was-

Hughes: I’m sure somewhere, somebody else experienced that.

Hunn: It wasn’t like that with corn, if they were just filling well, they didn’t start doing that later, because we’ll because they got the machines while they would actually shell the corn in from the field. But with the solids, they were just solids.

Hughes: You know when the stuff was burning, it wasn’t fun.

Hunn: Especially when it got to be sour.

Hughes: Yeah.

Hunn: You get fumes in it.

Hughes: Yeah those fumes, were strong. Especially on a hot day, but that’s you would, you know, what you had to do. I never knew how exactly he got his farm.

Hunn: Its one or two black owned farms in Lincoln County. I don’t know if those people left their home though. I don’t remember.

Hughes: Yeah

Hunn: I know there’s one after Johnny.

Hughes: Johnny V?

Hunn: Yeah, Johnny V. Then there is the Cucchi out there in [ Bonnieville/ or Bowling Green]

Hughes: Yeah.

Hunn: So not many of us.

Hunn: Simpson that’s where they, the Cucchi.

Hughes: There’s not many.

Hunn: I can’t think of anyone else, anyone back who was a farmer.

Hughes: My grandfather owned one in Parkston. You know, they had a, the story goes, that my great great grandfather whose name is Bruce Green had obtained a farm right in Parkston, in the city of Parkston. And they wanted him out of there, really bad. I don’t know, this is what my nana told me and they really wanted him out of there, they didn’t want a black man owning property. And they wanted the ground to build a school. To which he had about two or three acres of farm, so they gave him nine to ten acres out on Wilson. Just to get him. That how they ended up with a big farm you know, just to get him out. And then the Marshalls, they kind of, it was a big part. It was about nine acres on the Bruce side and then ten or eleven on the Marshal side. So that was a big area. You know, it’s a lot of land back in the day, you know, for black folks own.

Hunn: In Turnersville, my grandfather, well of course, his grandfather, my great great grandfather, he owned the most land in that area. Most of the other black people come to lived there. Well Mr. Bruce* owned about twelve but actually Joe Brown he owned about twelve acres but all the rest of them were owned by my grandpa.

Hughes: Black folks owned a lot—

Hunn: Owe a lot next the road, there was a lot of acre, half acre lots, that black people owned. But, I was always told that the property was bought from my grandfather. My great grandfather.

Shenton: So did your grandfather hire people to work on land too?

Hunn: I don’t know. See my father, my grandfather died before I was born. He died twenty years before I was born.

00:49:04 Zamarripa: Oh.

Shenton: Okay. Hunn; He was born in 1918 to 1959.

Hunn: Like Mike said, you know, everybody helped one another. You would be working on this farm one day, and if there’s no food, you go to the next farm. So, everybody got it done.

Shenton: That’s not for getting paid though?

Hughes: Nah.

Hughes: Those are details of a swap.

Hun: That’s a swap

Hughes: They would go to church lets say Sunday, well you would say, I need to get this done, we would be able to help out on your farm, and you know, lets say this week, we do this work  on your farm, and then next week then we go—Two days over here, two days over there. Everybody worked together, you know.

Hunn: You know, they also raised buildings, barns, or something. Everybody gets together to raise that building.

Hughes: If we’re going to put up a barn, we are going to raise this barn. Something like the Mennonites with what the stuff they do now, the same concept.

Hunn: And the women would have quilting parties.

Hughes: Yeah.

Hunn: They made quilts. A bunch of women would get together and make quilts.

Hughes: I’m going to tell you another thing- but I’m going to have to go in a little bit. There was a lot of bootlegging and homemade whiskey. And stuff—

Zamarripa: [Laughter]

Hunn: You got that right!

Hughes: You got them corn whiskey and stuff; you know it was some of the best.

Hunn: [Laughter]

Zamarripa: And no one got in trouble?

Hughes: Yeah, yeah.

Hunn: Nope. It was all home-brewed.

Hughes: And the people watched out. I know a story from them people from Wilsonville [KY]. This one place that bootlegged—sold whiskey, and they got raided four or five times and never found- never. Because one house was on the corner, and his house was down the road. And they knew and looked out for when they were coming. From what I understand, he had a trap door in his house that was covered up.

Zamarripa: Smart man.

Hughes: By the time they when knew who was coming, they got raided three or four times, and they never found the stash. I heard this from one of the older ladies because she was kin to—related to the person. But she had said he had to watch out. The way his land was set up, they had to get through some other houses to get to his house. By the time they got to his house- you know. My point was that people, even in farming and whatever else they did—watched out. They looked out for each other.

Hunn: Yeah, on my mother’s side of the family, most of her people were bootleggers. Even down to the women.

Hughes: Oh yeah.

Hunn: I had a cousin, first cousin; and all she ever did was bootleg. She was raised because her daddy would bootleg. My daddy would bootleg, too, I know he did. And all my uncles were bootleggers. I had one uncle, uncle ray*, who would make his own, every summer he would make peach brandy or peach brander** and then he would take whiskey barrels and soak up that peach brandy in them whiskey barrels. Somebody was always stopping by at that trailer. He had a trailer.

Zamarripa: [Laughter]

Hughes: Who was that tale a preacher that preached in the black church and he would say, “ Open them doors, and open them wide and let the voices of the lord travel from outside.” Then he would bend down and get a little shot.

Zamarripa: [More Laughter]

Hughes: He would preach a little long and say it again.

Hunn: I would and need a little shot from the son of the gin. [Laughter]

Hughes: Open the doors and open them wide and the forces of the lord travel outside. Somebody got mad at him and when he came up for the last time he said, “ Close them doors and close them quick; some son of a gun is going to throw a brick.” Hughes, Hunn, Zamarripa: [Cracking up in laughter]

Hunn: Somebody is going to say someone stole their shot.

Hughes: Yeah….

Hughes: I got to call my wife; I have some appointments to attend.

Hunn: All right.

Hunn: Pretty much all of it.

Hughes: What he told you, I did. And I back him up--

Hunn: Well you had a lot to say.

Hughes: We’ve been there.

Hunn: They would come and get you.

Zamarripa: They come to get you or find you?

Hunn: They would come and ask you if you wanted to do some work.

Shenton: Did they send people to the communities to ask or did they?

Hunn: Most of the time, the farmer would be coming around and looking for them.

Shenton: So, actually, the owner would come by—

Hunn: You would know just about time, as to what crops were ripening to be harvest. So, you know, they would be lookin to come get you and ask if you could help. If you did one haul to another then you would then do them do the line. You would help them and they would help you too.

Shenton: When you say white sharecroppers, those were not landowners, and they were working on someone else land too and need help? Or were they landowners who were asking people to come and worked their land? I’m just trying to figure out who owns what-

Hunn: The white sharecroppers, they would end up sharing their crops with us, a portion of the proceeds from the land, or whatever they were contracted to do. Most of them had contracts to do certain things.

Shenton: So, they were working a part of someone else land and they would come to you or hire you all to help them then they would pay you a wage, but they were getting a percentage of the crop, is that right?

Hughes and Hun: Right, right...

Shenton: So you have the landowner, the sharecropper, and the workers were three different things?

Hunn: Yeah, right, right

Hughes: Sort of a straw boss type of thing. It’s what you would call a straw boss.

Shenton: And the straw boss was also a sharecropper?

Hunn: Yeah

Hughes: Yeah, something like that, or it could be one of two things; sometimes, the straw boss could be someone running the crew. Straw boss could be that person that was getting paid to hire out workers.

Shenton: And he was usually making a percentage on the crop, right?

Hunn: Or he was getting paid himself.

Hughes: Or being paid for being on them, to run the crew.

Shenton: Got it. So it was all the way down the chain, is that right.

Hughes and Hunn: Yeah, yeah [ in agreement]

Shenton: Interesting. All right, well thank you all so much.

Zamarripa: Thank you so much.

Hunn: No problem

“growing up as an only child was good. They kept me busy.”

Transcript

Charlotte King: Hello. My name is Charlotte King. Today I am interviewing Lucretia Banks, who is living in Cincinnati currently, but was born and raised in Danville, Kentucky. I am here with Dr. Shenton Rothavy Kong and DeCarlos Slaughter. Today I am interviewing Mrs. Banks, who is currently living in Cincinnati. We are recording this interview in Danville, below [the] county African American Historical Society. In today's interview, we will be talking about your experiences during urban renewal in Danville, Kentucky. So to start off, could you tell us a little bit about your family?

Lucretia Banks: I'm an only child. My parents divorced when I was six years old, so we moved in with my grandfather. So that's the way that was. That's about my family, we were a close family. My mom has a sister and a brother, and neither one of them had children. So I was an only child all the way around. You know, my aunt didn't have any children and neither did my uncle.

Charlotte King: What was it like being an only child for you? Was it an easy experience? Or did you have any like hardships?

Lucretia Banks: You know, growing up as an only child, that's all I knew. So it didn't bother me at all. But as you get older and you have no one to help you fight your battles, then it's like, oh, I needed a sister or brother, but growing up as an only child was good. They kept me busy. My grandparents lived in Harrisburg, Kentucky. I would go [in] the summers and spend [time] there and with my great, great grandpa, we called him grandpa Jim, so it was a good life. They kept me busy.

Rothavy Kong: So, Mrs. Banks, so you said that you're an only child, and as an only child too, I feel like I can relate to your experience when you [said] that it's kind [of] lonely and, you know, growing up, no one's like backing you up and stuff. So what do you like to do in your free time, you know, as an only child, and what do you like to do for fun?

Lucretia Banks: Okay. As an only child growing up, of course, I was always more or less around older people. I was born, actually and raised on Russell Street. At that time, it wasn't a lot of children on Russell Street, so I was around mostly older people. We would sit around [with] older ones, you know, you learn how to crochet and embroidery and then [whatever] kids [were] there you played [with], and they used to have, one of the neighbors, Paul had a game croquette, I think that's what you called it. So we would play that, and he had a little clubhouse that girls were not allowed in. It was only for boys. So it was, it was good, you know, playing the piano and that kept me busy.

Decarlos Slaughter: Hi, Mrs. Banks. This is decarlos, so you said growing up, obviously, as the only child and stuff, you didn't have anybody there as you [get] older, like, [to] back you up when it comes to the sense of siblings. So with that being said, How was the community during the time you were growing up?

Lucretia Banks: Okay, the community was good. Basically, you know, that's the way it was. It was a close knit community. The neighbors and all were very helpful, considerate [and] respectful. We respect our elders. We had to, everyone had to have a handle. It was either Mr, Mrs or cousin or aunt or something, you know, you just couldn't say Mary, you know. So it was good and like I said, they kept us busy but I loved our Russell Street and so it was right up on the corner was College Street, you know. So when Centre College and we'll have a game, the lights would come on and it would light up half the end of Russell Street. Russell Street, growing up for me, was a good street because we had teachers that lived on Russell Street. We had pastors that lived on Russell Street. JW Smith, the taker at that time, lived on Russell Street. Mr. Montague Adams, he was a furniture maker and stuff, and a lot of the men on Russell Street work for the railroad. Let me see what, oh, we had beauticians, You know, hair salons, I guess I would, I should say hair salon and it was just a nice area growing up.

Charlotte King: So Mrs. Banks, how did you, so did you feel like the strong cultural and community support helped you growing up when, if there were like, any struggles you went through, did you feel like you were safe and supported?

Lucretia Banks: I would say this, if there [were] any struggles, I didn't know anything about them, like I said, the community was the community. You know, they kept us kids busy, like I said, we had, you know, you can go almost to anyone to get help from and, you know, it was good.

Charlotte King: So could you tell us a little bit about any like, social or cultural differences within the atmosphere, if you've noticed any between here in Danville and there in Cincinnati?

Lucretia Banks: Well, Cincinnati is totally different [from] Danville. You know, a lot of things [have] changed for me in Danville when I'm there now it's like, Oh, I'm so upset they took the stoplight down and I grew up with the stoplight and then, of course, everybody talked about [the] second street. I remember, you know, Second Street. I couldn't go to Second Street in the bars, I guess you would say, because I was young, but I remember the joy and the fun that you would see, that people would have. We [were] busy, you know, we go to the ballpark, have fun there. Of course, when I left Danville, blacks, of course, didn't have good jobs, per se, like the telephone company or whatever they have. Now, I don't know, because I moved out of danville in [the] 1966. I graduated in 1962 and so things [have] changed, and it's good to see the change when I'm there, but a lot of the memories that I had growing up are gone.

Rothavy Kong: So when you mentioned [that] you graduated in 1962 so I just want to ask, like, what was your school experience like during your time?

Lucretia Banks: oh, going to school. I started to [go to] school in 1950 and I went to Bate the whole time from 1950 to 1962 and it was, it was good. I had no problems in school and I don't think we students gave teachers any problems either, that I know of. The School, of course, kept us busy as much as they could. I remember, you know, most of my teachers, although they're deceased now, but it was good. I didn't know any difference, you know. So as far as going to Bate school, I loved it. I loved the people there. I loved the teachers there. I know when, years ago, when Sports [were] going on, we had to play football or something like that. We would have to leave Bate school and go to Danville High School and use their field to play Football. I was a cheerleader and so we did what we had to do. I don't know, but a lot of people talk about the bun boy and so when we would get out of school, at Bate, we would stop at the bun boy and of course, we had to enter through the back, and so we did that for years. And also, I don't know if the Bun boy ever integrated, and I have no idea they may have after I moved out of Danville, I don't know, but you know, we basically had fun, enjoyed life. You know, people were educated minded. Scholarships [weren't] going on at that time, a lot of people could not afford to go to college. Those that could did go as far as I know. You know, I still love Danville. It's just all that I remember is gone and all I have is memories. You know, it wasn't a bad place, you know, because I don't think it was any, you know, it wasn't a whole lot of racial issues growing up. In my time. I don't think you know, you just did what you needed to do and kept it moving.

Rothavy Kong: So I can see that when you're talking [that] you love Danville, but they take away your memories and when you mentioned [that] you love the stop light, but then they took it away.

Lucretia Banks: Right there, what wanted and second street, it's like, when I was down there for a funeral, it was like, oh my god, I grew up with this stop light and then they took it down there, and stop sign there, right there. What is it? Constitution Square.

Rothavy Kong: I think that Danville, like many other places you know, have undergone significant urban renewal effects. So how do you see these changes affecting your family and also your community as a whole?

Lucretia Banks: Well, you know, it's not, like I said, I'm 80 now, so I'm okay, you know, it's just you adjust to where you are and so of course, you know, Cincinnati is totally different than Danville is, but it depends on what areas you live on and I mean areas that you live in, because different parts of town are different than others and stuff like that. Danville was, and seemed like Danville was just a whole community. You know, it's just that we walked a lot. A lot of people didn't have a car. Going to school, sometimes people would come up Fourth Street, I think, and boy, we were all piling the car on top of each other with books and everything on my way to school. It's just different. But I didn't. I grew up basically in Danville, Kentucky. So when I moved out of Danville, I moved down, and you know, it was a different life.

Decarlos Slaughter: Earlier you spoke about the bun boy, I was hoping that you could elaborate more and explain what that is quickly?

Lucretia Banks: Okay, the Bun boy was standing close to Bate school, Stanford Avenue, I think, or, you know, wherever it was.
