And for me, I was the type of person that I would walk home after the game and just, you know, but I'd be ready to practice the next the next day, I would just, I was busy trying to be the grandson that my grandmother wanted us to be.

43:06 So this may be kind of like a thicker question, but when other cities are approaching urban renewal, do you think there are any lessons they can learn when looking at how Danville did urban renewal?

43:41 I wish I could teach financial literacy. I do, because I'm an investor. That's one of the angles that I go at when talking to people that look like myself, is I try to tell them, don't give away your grandmother's house, you know. I like to point people, people, especially your age and and before you got to be freshmen, because I believe that life can change so much from the decisions you make your first year to a college and right out of high school. And I just wish wish that more people period, just new financial literacy, so that they're not taken advantage of, because there's, there's even strength in numbers, you know, if a couple people in the neighborhood know something that can keep it from being developed, if I knew what I knew now, we probably wouldn't have a 32 unit apartment complex, you know, next to me. But people don't know how to get their hands on money. They don't know how to use leverage. And sometimes, sometimes people don't understand that. Sometimes it's best just to make sacrifices for a common goal.

45:17 I kind of, you know, I wish they wouldn't have came through and did it. But even as I'm currently today working on some projects in another county in an area this similar to mine, that is what I try to pass on to them, because even I don't have the answer for what to do when you hold on to a house that can no longer be fixed and your family no longer has the money

45:48 to renovate the house, I try to present solutions, but, financial literacy is to me, is the starting point of even knowing what you got.

46:08 So you know what to do when somebody makes an offer on your property, or how you can keep something like that in your family.

46:23 So you said that you would want to talk to kids like our age, like freshman year, right out of high school. Besides financial literacy, is there anything that you would want to specifically tell them or teach them?

46:49 Number one thing is just that I just know that the that, the way things are just your your first couple years in college, that first credit card is offered to you. I mean, that makes a big difference. Do you understand credit utilization? You know? Do you understand how your score comes about? That can make a difference in five years, whether you're setting a stoplight with somebody that looks through the car just like yours, and you're paying 200 more dollars for yours because your freshman year, you took that $1,000 card and ran it up. And I think that a lot of people don't understand the way that works, the way these things Snowball from not knowing how to utilize credit. And I just think that it can make for decisions that you're paying for long after your freshman year in college or graduating from high school. So that's the biggest thing, because when you hear people say they can't do something, you know, like just say, for instance, when my wife said to me, when she says, I can't afford to put money in my 401k if you've never seen anybody that look like you, or told you anything like that, or you've never seen what happens from, you know, from investments compounding, You just you can never understand the value of that. And to me, you know, not bragging, but I like to say that, you know, some of the things I think I did is just things that normal people do or that, you know, I didn't study, I just took advantage of the opportunities that were there. And you know when, when, uh, when the check came. I never looked at it like I need to get that money back. I just said, let me find a way to go make more money. And then I also believe in something else, which is, if you study something every day for five years, you are the expert. I mean, you're and most of the time, when people are asking you about stuff, if you've been looking at for the past five years, what you know is what's what's happening right now, and that, and that sometimes is good enough to to put you in the game. I mean, it's good to know the history of what things did 20 years ago. But if you know what's been going on in whatever line of business, or whatever you've been in the past five years, you're you're in the game.

49:32 So did you know what you wanted to do right out of college, in the Marine Corps? Well, I

49:38 in college, I thought I want to be electrical engineer, but I think I was just doing what, you know, I thought college was something you just supposed to go to and something you're supposed to do. I mean,

49:50 my high school career is, is, is, easily, I did enough to get by, but I, Repeat your question again, I'm sorry I got sidetracked.

50:03 I was just saying, like, did you know what you wanted to do right out of college in the Marine Corps.

50:10 Out of the Marine Corps I didn't have any big aspirations. I just knew I wanted. I just know I wanted to come home and work. We just, we made, we made good money. And I know that sounds like the bar is low, but when I, when I came home and I had a chance to work at a place like Toyota or Corning, and Toyota was kind of just starting up then. To be able to make that kind of money, and as a single guy, and then when my father passed, excuse me, he left me a little bit of something. And just not needing, not needing that much money, I just have that much, you know, big aspiration. But I think that, you know, later on, when I got to, I don't know, in my 40s or something, I always knew I wanted to try to retire at at 55 and that was a whole, to be honest, that was a whole goal with the 401k the real estate thing just happened to come in and and I just looked at money differently once I saw that paying off debt, then it could give me my time back. And that's the most important thing you ever have, is your time.

51:34 How was it like navigating your career, like deciding whether, like, you're gonna stay in, I think you said Japan, to, like, keep working there, or to come back here, and then, like, getting into real estate.

51:48 It was easy. I knew I was coming back, but if I didn't say, what led me to go is we were working for probably I'd been the maintenance shop for about 15 years, and the whole 15 years we had been working 50 hours a week, and then one day they come in and said, You guys are working 40 hours a week, and and what management would tell you is they'd say, you know, the extra 10 hours a week is overtime, But if you worked at for 15 years, most of us live paycheck to paycheck, so when all of a sudden, you took me back, I was 20% behind on my bills, and they said, literally to the maintenance department that we're going to see them 40 hours and until somebody goes to to nothing, nobody wanted to go. But they needed some help over in Japan, and until people signed up, they were just acting like it wasn't going to be there. Ironically, I felt like as soon as the wheels went up on the plane, they went back to 50 hours. But, but went to Japan and it was, it was, it was eye opening, you know. I mean, it's a different kind of camaraderie, not an us against them mentality, but it was just, you know, some of your co workers, and you become close in a different way when it's just you all. So that was just fun. But, I mean, I won't kid you, I went for the money more than I did the cultural experience. But of course, we had weekends and holidays off like everybody else, so we did get to experience some of it. But I'd be kidding you if I said, if I didn't say, the whole reason I went was for money. I mean, I remember when I went over, I told myself, because I start paying down credit card debt. I said I won't pay anybody interest anymore. I mean, of course, that's not true today, because I learned how to do different. But once I started paying off debt, I just was like, I'm not paying anybody interest anymore.

53:56 I guess so kind of switching gears a little bit. So you said that your first grade teacher was a woman of color?

54:03 She actually was a substitute. I kind of misspoke. My first grade teacher's name was Miss Burke, so Burke's bakery. Yeah, her name was, her name was Miss Burke, but the lady, her name was Lola Dale, but she was my grandmother's cousin. And it's just anytime you could see somebody at that time, to see somebody you could see at your own house, and then see him as a school teacher. You know when I'm not kidding you, probably until I got to middle school, was probably the next time I saw and as I told you, I said, Mr. Atkins, and he may have been one of those teachers in seventh grade. That doesn't mean I couldn't get by, because we had second third grade teachers. They loved us to death. You know, genuine, genuinely felt loved. I didn't feel like they put on or nothing. I felt like our second and third grade teachers, they just love to teach kids. They love kids. Yeah, so, but yeah. And then my next door neighbor, her name was Margaret Helm, and she taught at old Bate. So when my mother lived on Swope drive, she was there, so I had kind of influences around like that. And because my grandmother always had me at her side, at church and meeting people in the community is just a certain way that she had us address our elders and and it was just, to this day, I'm still like, Yes, ma'am, no sir. And I believe that stuff works, you know? I mean, I think that and work is the wrong word, but I just, I just think that that that's just something that that that we've lost. I think the just because you say yes sir or no sir to somebody doesn't mean that that they won't respect you. I think you give respect, you get respect.

56:02 You say you went to Bate, correct? Mhmm. How was your time at Bate? It was great.

56:06 The reason why I say this because Bate Middle School had probably been built two or three years before I went, so everything was still relatively new. So imagine starting in a newer School, where the gym floors new the lockers are new, and, you know, the carpets new, I mean, it was just and then also where we had three elementary schools, I meant Jenny Rogers and Toliver. It was a blending, so, really an eye opening experience all together. Because, as I said, not that it made it better, but the black people that on the west side of Danville kind of grew up a different way than some of the people on the south side. We were all back together. We probably had some some of the same experiences, but for some of us, it was the first time, chance where we actually blend, you know, we blend it all in together. So to me, I mean, it was different about that. I mean, never thought about, I remember, actually thought I wanted to play an instrument in the sixth grade. I mean, you want to do everything then, not only that, I'd say that's also when the experience of what you were came in, you know, I guess in elementary school, you probably wore whatever, nobody said anything. But once we got to to middle school, it made a difference if you had Levi's on or Converse or whatever, you know, I guess socially, things became a little different.

57:47 I always kind of have one question when I've done different interviews, not relating to Danville or anything, but if you could give us one piece of advice, like the most important piece of advice you would share with somebody, what would it be?

58:17 I believe that a lot of us have the ability to do a lot of things that we think we can't. I think that you have to become your own coach. I believe in routine. I know that's more than one thing, but I just I'm self driven, and I know maybe not everybody else has that in it, but I think it's really easy if you can look within yourself. I think a lot of us have greatness within us. I think that with the way information is made available to us today, I believe you can research and learn about a lot of things, and give yourself a lot of opportunities that people younger than me didn't see. And it's kind of maybe a little difficult for me sometimes, because the saying is, you know that I got it out the mud, and I believe that that's possible for other people, because even to this day, I don't know very many people that I can turn around and say, Hey, I need advice. Can you tell me how you do this? Because there's not too many people that look like me that have done it that way. So, I believe in research, and I believe in being self driven, and I believe in getting out of the bed and being ready to go before you start your day. That's, that's, that's, that's what I know because that's what I've done. Thank you. I wanted to ask, you talked a lot about the past, I wanted to ask you a little bit about the future. Especially the future of community here in Danville. So you talked a lot about community. What do you see as the future for your grandkids? What is what is life going to be like for them, either here in Danville or somewhere else in 20 years? Do you think? Well, I won't lie. The biggest thing I'm concerned about is because of what I do now is housing. I worry about housing for people that look like me today, even though I rent to everybody but I worry about what it's like to have a future where you don't actually have your own place. It takes up such a significant amount of money, and we all have to have money to be able to live our lives. So just housing and financial literacy, that's what I worry about with with my my kids and grandkids the most. But the one thing is, I can't say that, you know, they'll get it the same way I did, but I'll tell them the same thing that I that I told you all, is that, you know, regardless of how you do in school, I believe that there are other ways around it. I'm not discounting education. I think education is important, important for for those that choose to make that part of their life. But I definitely want my grandkids to know that it is not the end all be all for being able to financially support yourself and your family. Do you think community will look different for your grandkids than it did for you. I hope so. I really do. I think that our town is growing. I can't remember what the population was growing up, but we grew up literally, I would say like it's older now, of course, but when I was younger, most of the black people in the community knew each other. I mean, you just, you could say, whose kid is that? And we kind of knew each other. I think the community has changed. I think it's, it's grown. I think we don't. I mean, everybody doesn't know each other. I think, you know, Centre College has grown, but I actually am excited about the growth of our, of our town, you know, just hoping that, you know, there are people that don't get left behind within that growth. But I'm, I'm hopeful that that some of the things that I spoke of that we had in the 80s, that it's not that I think that that I have a lot of that we have a lot of bad experience or interaction, but I just wish that those days could come back to where our sense of community was a lot closer than I mean, I say that mean when we paid, we played football, our businesses in town, I mean, they all kicked in. I mean, you didn't have to worry about school programs not having money, and things like that. I mean, there was a sense of pride to where you felt like the people in the community actually felt like, I think they actually took pride in knowing that they were helping shape the next generation. Thank you for sharing all that. Just to finish up, is there anything you haven't talked about or anything that you wanted to talk about that you wanted to say, No, I haven't. I appreciate you all giving me the opportunity to to talk a little bit about the past. I hope that that other people in the community will agree or like some of the things I've said. I hope that that I just, I just hope that we all stick together and just grow as a community. And I. I just live life happily ever after. We really appreciate your time. Thank you so much. Thank you. Thank you. Thank you.

Margaret Alcorn

00:24:09 LaJoy Spears Oh, you tried.

00:24:10 Miss Margaret Alcorn Ohh, I'd tried that. They used to have at Danville high school they would have class and you could go through that free of charge. I went to that three times and still ain't got no license.

00:24:29 Nick Minton That never -- that didn't exist for me when I was in high school, they quit offering, yeah.

00:24:34 LaJoy Spears They took it out of a lot schools. Yeah, we, we always thought you just wanted, you always had a good husband to take you. Where you needed to go, yeah.

00:24:43 Miss Margaret Alcorn I did, I did, but I would love to have had my license done, but, I didn't get them.

00:24:54 Nick Minton Was this common for a lot of women around your age?

00:25:05 Miss Margaret Alcorn To have em? Mmhmm. As if I was done out of school and married and had a job. And went and seen it in the paper where they just going to give free drive license driving test. So I went back to work and told these girls about it. It was about three of us. Four counting myself. And do you know everyone got their license and I'm still sitting here. I got none.

00:25:37 Nick Minton What did you do for your job?

00:25:40 Miss Margaret Alcorn I was in dietary at Ephriam McDowell. You know, 35 years. So I did a little bit of everything in dietary, that you would do in dietary. And that dietary’s the food part.

00:25:58 Nick Minton Could you run through maybe what you did on a regular day?

00:26:03 Miss Margaret Alcorn Well, I come in. Clock in. Got to clock in. Put your apron on. Wash your hands, put your hair net on. And prepare… most time I have prepared breakfast, lunch and supper. But my last thing was I was on the night shift. And we, that's when they had done opened up the cafeteria at night. We used to didn't have that. I would bake all night. And then open up the cafeteria. Stayed there to about 5:00 in the morning. It was a busy time. Cook the supper for the patients and the cafeteria.

00:27:36 Dr. Shenton So you talked a little bit about the E-lite cab company on Main Street. What other establishments do you remember going to in the 1950s and 60s downtown in Danville? What other, what other places did you go to?

00:27:53 Miss Margaret Alcorn We had a restaurant on the corner. Where you can go in and get sodas, milkshakes, hamburger, hot dog. Then we had a pool hall. That was mostly for the men. I don't remember seeing no women going into the pool hall. We had a grocery store. Shoe shop get your shoe shine. Then you come out across the street and you had a another little restaurant. What's that other place over there now? Like uh, what's the name of that? The other little place on 2nd St. across from the Constitution Square.

00:29:10 LaJoy Spears & James Hunn The McDowell house.

00:29:11 Miss Margaret Alcorn [LaJoy Spears Yeah,[McDowell] McDowell. The house. [The museum.] OK, that was there. Did I say barbershop? I guess that’s about it. Like 8. I guess it'd be by 16 on the whole block.

00:29:41 Dr. Shenton Do you remember the names of any of the establishments?

00:29:50 Miss Margaret Alcorn The name of the establishments... I knew the cab was the E-lite. The grocery store was a Singleton. I believe it was Singletons. The pool hall. What was the name of the pool hall? I don't know what the name of the pool hall was.

00:30:19 LaJoy Spears I think it was mentioned in one of the other interviews for sure.

00:30:23 Dr. Shenton I think there two. There might have been two pool halls on 2nd St.

00:30:28 Miss Margaret Alcorn It might have been 2.

00:30:30 Dr. Shenton [Margaret] Tibs maybe [Who, tibs?] and I think there was an E-lite pool hall too was there.

00:30:43 Miss Margaret Alcorn Taxes, that was E-lite. If I can remember…

00:30:57 Dr. Shenton So you've described you're describing now the places that that you feel like you would have been welcomed into that were owned by other African American entrepreneurs on 2nd St. and on Main Street. Were there other places that that would have been a different story in downtown in the 1950s and 60s?

00:31:14 Miss Margaret Alcorn Oh yeah, you had the Bun Boy. (Can you guys help out over there?)

00:31:30 LaJoy Spears These are places you knew you weren't welcome Aunt Margaret?

00:31:34 Miss Margaret Alcorn Yeah, that's what I'm talking about. Had to go to the back door. Bun Boy and Begley’s. You didn't sit down in there.

00:31:44 LaJoy Spears What about the Hub Frankel?

00:31:46 Miss Margaret Alcorn The Hub Frankel, you can go in there.

00:31:48 LaJoy Spears You could go in there? You were always welcome in there?

00:31:50 Miss Margaret Alcorn There, but most time you went to the basement[ of Hub Frankel.

00:31:56 LaJoy Spears [Of the Hub Frankel] Now where the garments the same?

00:32:07 James Hunn And then there was the job. That job addressing on Stanford Rd. To the lake. 50’s & 60’s.

00:32:15 Miss Margaret Alcorn ABC grill.

00:32:21 LaJoy Spears Where would you go for date night? Did you and your husband ever go out for date night?

00:32:26 Miss Margaret Alcorn No. Was, no date night. Date night at home. That's the thing of the new.. that's the new thing. Date night. [ No date night.

00:32:36 LaJoy Spears [Going out is new?] You had date nights at home. What did date night at home look like?

00:32:46 Miss Margaret Alcorn Sitting around playing with the children. Cookin’ supper.

00:32:57 LaJoy Spears Aunt Margaret, were there any family photos or videos that were passed down to you that gave you a bit of history about the family?

00:33:08 Miss Margaret Alcorn My Alcorn family.

00:33:12 LaJoy Spears Ohh you got stuff. Ohh.

00:33:19 Miss Margaret Alcorn That's on my husband's side.

00:33:24 LaJoy Spears Well, they kept some.

00:33:25 Miss Margaret Alcorn They had on my husband's side. We have a Thanksgiving reunion. All get together. This is one of our books that we put out. Used to be, we would have every Thanksgiving we would have a book like this. I got pictures and different ones.

00:33:55 LaJoy Spears So your book will talk about accomplishments in the year and everything in the family tree and things like that? That's really nice.

00:34:06 Miss Margaret Alcorn Got there…

00:34:08 LaJoy Spears Oh wow.

00:34:09 Nick Minton Are those government documents?

00:34:10 Miss Margaret Alcorn His grandparents and… Parents, and this is all of us.

00:34:17 LaJoy Spears That's nice… death certificates, birth certificates. Family trees.

00:34:27 Miss Margaret Alcorn Old family houses.

00:34:30 LaJoy Spears That's very rare, auntie. We don't have that on your other side, do you?

00:34:33 Miss Margaret Alcorn No, no, no.

00:34:35 Nick Minton This is her husband's side? OK…

00:34:39 LaJoy Spears Why did you all think that was important?

00:34:42 Miss Margaret Alcorn Well, my brother-in-law thought it was important. It's all been the family.

00:34:49 LaJoy Spears That's nice. Ya’ll check this out. What else? Will you tell us about your mother and father Aunt Margaret?

00:35:04 Miss Margaret Alcorn My mother was Matilda Young. She was from Springfield. My father was Sammy T Yao. He was from Perryville. Back then, some of them was married and some of them wouldn't. So you see two different names. You got a young and, and you got a Yao. That was my mother and my father.

00:35:36 LaJoy Spears What about reunions on your mother's side?

00:35:39 Miss Margaret Alcorn They have faded. It has faded out right now. So we haven't had none in the last five years.

00:35:51 LaJoy Spears You haven't had a family reunion.

00:35:52 Miss Margaret Alcorn We haven't had a family reunion. We have had gatherings, but not a family reunion.

00:36:02 Nick Minton Does being the matriarch of your family does that kind of influence you to want to bring those back?

00:36:13 Miss Margaret Alcorn Yes. Yeah. Yeah, I would love to have him back. I'm the only one left on my mother's side. Of the children, siblings. I got three sisters gone and got a brother. So I’m surrounded with nephews and nieces and great nieces and all of them. Yeah. I would love for it to come back.

00:36:53 Dr. Shenton Can you talk a little bit about how life was different for your parents than it was for you. The difference between your parents’ generation and what it was like growing up for you and Danville?

00:37:10 Miss Margaret Alcorn I'm saying it was really different. They both came to Danville, and when they started out, they must have been in Danville. Well, again, they've could have been in Perryville or they could have been in Springfield. So I'm not sure, nobody never talked about that, where they started from.

00:37:43 LaJoy Spears Yeah, I don't think I've ever heard you talk about that either.

00:37:51 Miss Margaret Alcorn We used to get together on Easter. Every Easter we will get together. All the siblings and their families. That rolled down. And I'll see my brother like once every 2-3 years. So my father had a… we call them, T mighty 4. T- where you had that little rubber seat sitting in the back. And he was the only one that had the (car) and drove. Everybody else had to -- You didn't get no, no license at no 16. You did good if you got with (unintelligible), you didn't get no license at no 16. So that was the difference. Daddy was the only one that had a car. Most time we had to walk to school. Walk everywhere you went.

00:39:26 Nick Minton Was that far for you?

00:39:30 Miss Margaret Alcorn No, not really, because I knew I had. To do it. That’s the only way I was going to get to school.

00:39:40 Dr. Shenton What kind of work did your father do?

00:39:41 Miss Margaret Alcorn Farming, he worked over. They used to have at KSD. They used to have a big farm over there, gardening and cows and all kinds of things. So he worked over there. My momma does… she do housework, going around town, doing, for the rich bunch. I have worked in the homes-- one time, she had a family that she worked for, and when she couldn't go out, I would go. And work and they were. Have workers. Doing different things. And you had to feed them, so you had to cook. So I guess that's why I started cooking. And then when my mom worked the night and she got off from work, I had to have supper ready.

00:40:53 Nick Minton So would you help your mother work? Whenever…? What age did you start helping her?

00:41:01 Miss Margaret Alcorn I'll say by 14.

00:41:04 Nick Minton And how long did you do that for?

00:41:08 Miss Margaret Alcorn Till I got married. All while was in school.

00:41:21 Dr. Shenton So the land that your father was farming on, that was who owned that land. Do you know it was on the campus of KSD and they owned it. And then how did that work with the? How how did that situation work? Was he farming for somebody else, or were they? Were they bringing that whatever they were farming back home, or how did? That work? No?

00:41:40 Miss Margaret Alcorn They use it at the school. Like when they get ready to cook, to go out, they'll bring it in. It's done already harvest, but they pick it and bring it in to the cooks. They would cook it.

00:41:58 Dr. Shenton So he was just being paid for doing the work on that land, is that? Right, got it.

00:42:07 Miss Margaret Alcorn They even had hogs over there.

00:42:18 Dr. Shenton Was there other… did you have a big garden growing up too? Was your dad gardening at home for the family too? Or was that? And what did that look like? What? How were you involved in that?

00:42:24 Miss Margaret Alcorn Mm-hmm. Mm-hmm. I wouldn't, just, I just looked at. But I'm sure I have some dealings in it. We had. We live here on Randolph Hill. You know where the Lee’s Chicken Famous Recipes, the street there. We lived out there and we will have a small garden in the backyard. Brought the country to the town and we have beans. We didn't have no corn, potatoes, peas, peppers, tomatoes. So I guess I had to pick them out. Pick them I didn't put them out, but I had to bring them in. That's the way it was with me and my husband. We always had a garden. We have one on Oak St. We had one on 4th Ave. Have one on Duggan Hill. Russell street. Now, I don't think we had on Russell Street. But that would help you out. What you made could go still with everything.

00:44:02 Dr. Shenton Did you have to do a lot of work preserving all that was? That a big job?

00:44:10 Miss Margaret Alcorn Yeah, I learned it from my mom. I preserve now. I just made some pear relish, but it got me, won’t do it no more.

00:44:24 Dr. Shenton It's a lot of work I...

00:44:25 Miss Margaret Alcorn There's a lot of work.

00:44:25 Dr. Shenton Think I knew a little better.

00:44:26 Nick Minton That my grandparents still do it and it's like, so strenuous sometimes.

00:44:35 LaJoy Spears And she shares it with the family. You're going to talk about Bate School?

00:44:44 Miss Margaret Alcorn Bates School was a good thing. When John W. Bates came through, and founded Bates School and it was a wonderful thing. All black. And then the integration came through. Then we had choice of going to Bates or going to Danvile High, so I chose to stay at Bates. I'm a bad alumni.

00:45:24 Dr. Shenton But give it to Michael Hughes who wanted to ask a question.

00:45:29 Michael Hughes What I wanted you to talk about is your involvement. Now that we're at school. Your involvement in the alumni association. I know right now he's still the President of the Alumni Association. When did actually form? Do you remember exactly when y'all started to form the Alumni Association?

00:45:55 Miss Margaret Alcorn I think it was 1967.

00:46:03 Michael Hughes 1967. So OK now what I want you to talk about is the legacy that Melvin, your husband created by doing the video. And do… did he this all of a sudden say, well, we need the video this? Or did it was this something that you guys talked about as an alumni, you know, you know?

00:46:27 Miss Margaret Alcorn No, it wasn't something that the alumni created, he did on his own.

00:46:31 Michael Hughes OK.

00:46:37 Miss Margaret Alcorn Just like a hobby for him. Yeah, he loved doing it.

00:46:42 Michael Hughes Because of that fact that he did create this even today. That video we have the videos of all the 89 and I think 86 reunions and stuff and it's still. Creates a legacy every time somebody sees it, we have, yeah, they have that on there. So I just wondered, I've always wondered what inspired him to do that.

00:47:11 Miss Margaret Alcorn Just a hobby. And he liked doing it.

00:47:17 LaJoy Spears Talk about your celebrations used to be a huge thing for the Bate reunions in town. So many people used to come, talk about when you got together and your gatherings, you had photographers, they had nice dinners, dances. Yeah, it was a big thing.

00:47:34 Miss Margaret Alcorn Yes, it was.

00:47:37 Nick Minton She's pulling out something.

00:47:37 Miss Margaret Alcorn Let me bring out my book. And let this talk for me.

00:47:42 LaJoy Spears What you. Got oh.

00:47:51 Nick Minton What is that?

00:47:54 LaJoy Spears I'll let you look through it because I can see it later. This is good stuff.

00:47:59 Michael Hughes We've got, we've got copies here.

00:48:02 Miss Margaret Alcorn You got them up here.

00:48:06 LaJoy Spears So who was the historian? There was someone that you had elected offices in the alumni association. Tell us about that Aunt Margaret.

00:48:16 Miss Margaret Alcorn We are Bates High School Alumni Association. We registered. And we used to meet like every two or three years, five years maybe. When we first started. It's probably in here somewhere, but.

00:48:43 Michael Hughes I think I think you went every three years.

00:48:48 Miss Margaret Alcorn And then we went down to every two years.

00:48:50 Michael Hughes And because if people were starting to pass on.

00:48:59 Miss Margaret Alcorn So it's been 2 years, it's been four years now. Soon we'll be and we have it on the even year. We have a chairman have a vice, secretary, treasurer. We don't have a historian. It's a nice affair. We bring here invite everybody, alumni. And that was a big thing at first. They wanted us to invite everybody. They went to Bates school, but that's not what alumni is. Alumni is somebody that has graduated from that school. So then we started letting them come in. Some did and some didn't. So it's still a big old upa, about it.

00:50:13 Michael Hughes I think it's like you say, alumni was a way of recognizing folks that did graduate from the high school compared to people that just want to come and be public as it got as the years went by, and like I said, people started to die out and you know, everybody became invited to it, you know, but, I just wanted to. Because I know you. That's a big part of your, you know, you know, I didn't know exactly what year it started, so it started. Actually, three years after Bates School. Yeah.

00:50:58 Miss Margaret Alcorn They closed and they closed in 64.

00:51:03 Michael Hughes Yeah. OK. That’s all I have.

00:51:08 LaJoy Spears So Mr. Bates was a leader in education. Do you have any other leaders that were prominent in the community at the time? That that shapes some that some thought leaders in the community around education, around religion, around service.

00:51:24 Miss Margaret Alcorn Hmm, I'm sure it was but…

00:51:30 LaJoy Spears Or someone else who was very influential on your life.

00:51:38 Miss Margaret Alcorn All the teachers was influenced. I was influenced by them. And I'm sure they was influenced by me.

00:51:52 LaJoy Spears Tell us about your favorite teacher and a story from school, Aunt Margaret, who is your favorite teacher?

00:51:58 Miss Margaret Alcorn My favorite teacher, I don’t think I had a favorite.

00:52:02 LaJoy Spears Didn’t have a favorite? You're cutting up?

00:52:06 Miss Margaret Alcorn But I had one, Miss Burton. But she never did teach me. But she was a favorite teacher. One of my favorite teachers.

00:52:16 Nick Minton What did she teach?

00:52:20 LaJoy Spears What Miss Burton teach?

00:52:20 Miss Margaret Alcorn She taught everything in like in the second in elementary, third grade.

00:52:29 LaJoy Spears 3rd grade teacher. Why was she your favorite? What did she exemplify?

00:52:38 Miss Margaret Alcorn My daddy, down from Perryville, where he came from. Used to be a one room school down there. That's where my sister, two of my sisters went to the one room school down in Perryville. They never… they came on up to Bate, but they didn't graduate. And my brother, he wouldn't know what was here in Danville. She was my, I would say my favorite.

00:53:18 LaJoy Spears Favorite teacher?

00:53:27 Miss Margaret Alcorn Yes, these books are here at the African American Historical Society. These all three, we had all three of these. We gave them away as gifts one year. And all the teachers is they all in there. We heard men and women. Had three, I think 3 principles. In my lifetime, I didn't have nobody but Mr. Summers. I didn't have a Bates in… what was the other man name? And Mr. Summers.

00:54:17 LaJoy Spears The principal?

00:54:24 Michael Hughes I know it too, but I…

00:54:29 Miss Margaret Alcorn 1964… So these books shows you everything.

00:54:43 LaJoy Spears Nice pieces of information in there.

00:54:47 Michael Hughes Good, Mr. Goodlow.

00:54:48 James Hunn Good low.

00:54:48 LaJoy Spears Mr. Goodlow.

00:54:51 Miss Margaret Alcorn That's the other principle. Good school. We have all had everybody, different ones have come out, got doctors. Lawyers. Teachers. Came out of Bate.

00:55:21 Dr. Shenton So we're already coming up on an hour of time here. Is there anything else that that you wanted to say about your experience, your life, your own history that we haven't talked about yet that like?

00:55:35 Miss Margaret Alcorn No, I think we done talked about it all.

00:55:38 Nick Minton I have a final question to ask. What do you see for the future of Danville and the black community here?

00:55:53 Miss Margaret Alcorn What do I see? I don't know. I don't know. What's the ahead of…? There's so much going on in the world now, so I don't know what, what to say?

00:56:32 Indy Rodriguez What? What do you want to see?

00:56:43 Miss Margaret Alcorn What do I want to see? I don't know. More jobs. For these young people. Getting this mess off the streets. It has torn up a whole lot of families. This clean up Danville. We need to get back into the religion like we used to have. Old time meetings. Tenant meetings. Street meetings. These young people turn their life around, give it over to the Lord.

00:58:41 Indy Rodriguez Are you going to end it? Well, I think this deserves to be set on record. Miss Margaret is wearing pink on pink. And she has earrings and a necklace. And her blush is fabulous and [nails are done. She has her bracelet and…

00:58:56 Nick Minton [Nail nails. Were great.

00:59:04 Indy Rodriguez I think that you look good and we needed to say.[

00:59:07 Nick Minton [I agree.

00:59:08 Indy Rodriguez Something about it. You're a stylish woman. We're fine. OK, now that that is said, I've said my piece.

00:59:18 Miss Margaret Alcorn Did I do good?

00:59:19 Multiple Speakers Yes, yes.

00:59:20 Dr. Shenton Thank you so much, Margaret, thank you.
maryhuston:
Mary Huston

“we were rich in many things. Uh, not money. [laughter] And, not where we lived. But, having a loving mother and somebody that really cared about you.”

Transcript

Indy Rodriguez: Hello, my name is Indy Rodriguez, and today I am interviewing Mary Huston, who lived in Danville during the 1960s. I am here with Jeff Shenton, Michael Hughes, Ayiana Ognan, Morgan Dawson, and Alexander Bentley. Today is November 16th, we are recording this interview in Danville, Boyle County, African American Society, and today we will be discussing Mary Huston’s experiences during urban renewal.

IR: So, my first question to you is, what do you want to share with us? I mean, during this time, during all of your life experiences -

Mary Huston: About?

IR: About your experience in Danville during the 1960s-

MH: I’ve been here all my life, pretty much, except for a few months here and there. And, uh. I retired from the Housing Authority of Danville after 25 years. And… that’s about it.

Morgan Dawson: So you grew up here in Danville, what was your childhood like? If you wanna talk about that.

MH: When I look back on it, I think about… My mother worked hard. She had 6 children, she did day work, um… And I looked at it as having a hard childhood, but it was actually not. I had to get a little bit older to realize that… we were rich in many things. Uh, not money. [laughter] And, not where we lived. But, having a loving mother and somebody that really cared about you. Yeah. So, that was… When I look back now, it was rich.

MD: Yeah.

IR: So, I wanna go back to when you said that you spent 25 years working for, like, the Housing Authority, can you tell me more about that? What that experience was like, um, what brought you there, why you stayed so long?

MH: It was like… Well actually, I felt like I was stepping into a different world. But… knowing that we were providing affordable, um, housing, and a lot of the people who came and applied had never, some of them didn’t even have bathrooms where they came from. They… they were different. They were having to go by a lot of rules, as everybody else does according to the government. And, um. It was a little hard, but. I… met a lot of people that I’m now still friends with. I met a lot of people who looked at housing as a stopover, a way to have cheaper rent, and then, um, save money and go on to be homeowners. Uh, safe place to raise their children. So it was… was a pretty good experience. But after 25 years, it was time to go. However, I’m working there part time now. [laughter]

MD: Were you working there during the, um, urban renewal movement here in Danville?

MH: Yes, I actually had a small home on Southern Avenue. And, so urban renewal came through and bought the house, and then I bought the house where I live now, I’ve lived in the same house for 40 years.

MD: Yeah. Did, um, urban renewal affect how you did your job, like, did it affect working in housing when they were moving everything around during urban renewal?

MH: No, I had already moved by the time that I went to, went there.

MD: Okay.

IR: Um, I do have some questions prepared. I asked to know a little bit about you, before coming. So, um, I heard that you went to college, um, and you graduated, and I was wondering if you’re a, um, first generation student? And if so, what was your –

MH: Yes.

IR: - college experience like?

MH: First generation, uh I was raising two children, and uh, I went to school at night. At Lindsey Wilson.

IR: Was it… difficult? In terms of –

MH: Very hard. [laughter]

IR: I assumed, yeah. I was asking –

MH: Very, very hard.

IR: [inaudible] But –

MH: At that time I actually worked two jobs. I actually worked at Centre College, for 11 years, um… in the Bingham Hall when it was brand new. And, I would leave there about 1 o’clock in the afternoon and go to work for Lindsey Wilson’s satellite campus, and then go and take classes at night.

IR: Did those experiences, um, change the way you view yourself or your community? In any way, shape, or form?

MH: No, I just wanted better for me. … And I thought it was worth it. And as it turned out, it was.

IR: So, I’m aware that you advocate for education, is that a correct statement to make? Would you say?

MH: Yes it is. [laughter]

IR: Okay. So, how do you feel the state of the community today is affecting black youth in terms of valuing education, and pursuing higher education? Here, in Danville. Um, and how is that different from when you grew up here?

MH: I started out at Bates school.

IR: I see.

MH: The all-black school. And when I went to… 9th grade I went to the Danville high school, which is down – was down on Warner Street where Norton’s Center is now. And then, um… integration, and, then to Danville High School. The current Danville High School.

IR: So, how was that transition from Bates to Danville like? Very, like, quickly pause cause that’s, you know, from an all-black school to integration, that’s different, I assume.

MH: I thought the teachers were different. Because, I think at Bates School, they push you to be all that you could be. And, they knew your parents. [laughter] And, um, actually when I, the first year I went to Danville High School when I had problems with algebra or whatever, I would catch another bus and go to Bates School, and be tutored by Mr. Summers who was the principal at that time. … So.

IR: Was that… how did that work, logistically? Like, did you have to miss school, or did you just spend your days – more and more school? I’m just curious, personally.

MH: No, I – I would stop there if I had questions, and he was really patient, and he helped me a lot. But, um. … They always push you to do better. Always better.

IR: Is there a reason why you didn’t go back? Did your parents or your family want you to stay at Danville High School?

MH: No. I wanted to go the first year. Yeah, just so I could get used to it and see what it was really like. … And it really wasn’t that much different, other than, I mean it was school. It wasn’t, it – the difference, I just thought was that… maybe it was because I had just met the teachers. And they really didn’t know me. And I was used to being at a school where… the teachers were pretty much like family.

MD: I had to move schools a couple times, so I know that feeling of having to re-adjust.

MH: Yeah. It wasn’t bad, you know, I’ve always liked school, I’ve always liked to read. Um. So that never – that part never really bothered me, but sometimes when I got stumped, I knew where to go.

MD: Yeah.

IR: You’ve done a lot in terms of advocacy in aca –

MH: I can barely hear you.

IR: I’m sorry. Said, you’ve done a lot in terms of just, your work here in Danville, um. Has that had any impact in how you view yourself within your community, especially in connection to your appreciation for education? Is there a specific impact you wish to have in your community, or you think you have had?

MH: No, I don’t – I don’t think so, other than, I mean, I – it’s important, that people be educated. And, um. I would do anything that I could to try to help any, any kid that – if I was able to help new math, I don’t know what I can do now. [laughter] But… I’d be willing to try. I had a grandson, and I went back to school just for a little while to be tutored to learn the new math. When he was like, in the third, fourth grade, you know. He didn’t seem to be able to get it, but he seemed to be able to tell me I was doing it wrong. [laughter]

IR: So you’ve always valued education, and it’s been a huge part of your life.

MH: I took some classes at Eastern, for, uh, child and family studies. Um. I’m toying around with the idea that I might go back and take a class at a time. Um.

IR: You should.

MH: Not sure. I’m 74 now, so I’m [inaudible].

IR: So? You should!

MH: I’m not sure.

MD: What are you thinking of taking? Or just the idea of going.

MH: Just to complete that degree.

IR: You should! There’s no better day than today. [laughter]

But we went to school never missed a day. Never missed a day. I don’t resent it I accepted people and I liked people I knew there was people that didn't like us but they know [not to run that by us] because my daddy would get him you know he worked all of all of the land and country but they was good. We had a lot of people that bought from my grandfather houses and built and they was nice. Some of them moved away went to Tennessee and that always remember us on my birthday. So I really didn't have that I didn't resent people I like people I accept people, all my life I have done that. You can like me or not like me, that's okay. I still will say hi. You know, I left here when I graduated from here, ‘52 I went to Cincinnati, and I'd go down the street and speak to people. And they knew I was there at my uncle's house, but they wouldn’t speak and I'd cry all the way down the street and back, you know, and daddy say quit that right now. You know, said speak anyway, that's they problem. They never know, they might have have a good friend. So I met nice people, I can't complain. I went to a black school. The teachers was my parents. I was one thing my moth- my parents told them. They are your care when they home then I care. But when they're in your care, you are responsible for you know, and then I got married when I graduated, and in ‘52 and at here. And Matt arrive. And when we went to Gary, Indiana and I had two boys there. And then I came back here one of ‘em graduated, came back here in the seventh grade, and went to Danville high thats where he went no he went to Bate first year, and then he went to Danville High. And the other one came back and went to Jennie Rogers School. But, you know, and see Phillip was a lot like me, he just love people. You know, you can speak or not speak, but he loved people, you know, and he loved history. I just really didn't take the time what past my parents always taught me to love everybody. You know, said some people you don't like that people don't like you. But, you know, love ‘em anyway. So I just didn't let people hurt me like that. I really didn’t. And I've worked with some lovely people. And some of them had moved to Florida, California, and they'd always write back to check on me. You know, that’s the type of person I am. I was raised like that. So, you know, I find I love Bate because Bate teachers was our parents when we was there. They was a parent. When we came home, our parents was our parents. And we had lovely people. I lived on Lexington road, I guess until I was until I left Bate school and got married.

Paul Smith 22:33 Not cutting you [off], but we can't overemphasize how much the teachers did for us.

Sarah Riffe 22:40 They was our parents

Paul Smith 22:41 They were I mean, they did not have an eight to five job. If we were having a problem and stuff like they would still work until we understood what, what's going on. And we could call even at their home, stop by their home and they would help. So I cannot overemphasize how important the teachers were back in those days.

Dr. Jeffrey Shenton 23:07 Can you talk about some of the specific teachers that you remember and what they did for you?

Paul Smith 23:15 Sure. Specifically, I remember (Sarah Riffe: William Shelmet) Professor Summers those were our principals. School teachers, Miss Maggie was my first teacher. And I remember when I saved up eighteen dollars and bought my first savings bond. And that was really a big thing that she taught me how, how to do that. Bertha Bowman is another. My career was formed by Berth, Bertha Bowman the, the (Sarah Riffe: She was my typing teacher), typing and, shorthand and administrative type teacher because through her teaching me how to type I went into service. And that was my career administrative. And I wound up [the senior list advisor] was responsible for the military men that particular base. So Bertha and [Sled] there is just so many of them

Paul Smith 24:37 The male teachers were role models. Professor Summers, Dr. Green, Professor Green EP Green, Mr. Green referred to us as Mr. and Miss. Yeah Mr. Smith howe’er he was a that taught me respect for others and we still to this day, know how to say thank you yes ma'am. No ma'am Yes sir. No sir. And that was all because of their teaching their training their upbringing

Charli Brusnahan 25:11 So, so that sense of mutual respect from elders, was that really important in forming – like – a mentorship type of relationship? (Paul Smith: Exactly) (Sarah Riffe: Very true) (Paul Smith: Very, very true right)

Paul Smith 25:27 Yeah they we were never too old to be told what our place was or that we were wrong. And when they got on us, they got on us but somehow and I know you've heard this 100 times before cell phones before a lot of telephones before [UPS] or whatever way you get information When I messed up in, on the street My dad knew about it before I got home (Sarah Riffe: Word of mouth parents, everybody was your parents) Everybody was our parent so you get double punishment you know it, it was coming if Mr. Ryan had to break my butt or correct me on the street or let my [dad] know that I was somewhere with a bottle in my head that I wasn’t supposed to have or something when I got home daddy said “how did you like that drink. I hope you enjoyed it, come here I got something for you” (Sarah Riffe: Yeah He’d tell on you thats for sure) so, yeah

Paul Smith 26:38 But everybody back then I know you've heard this too it took a village to raise a child we had our village you know it don’t matter which street you was on they knew who you were, they knew who your parents were, they knew whether or not you was doing something you shouldn't do. (Sarah Riffe: Yeah they’d tell you go home in a minute its time for you to go home, Parents) . Seems like now the parents are not like they were back in those days. They want to be friends with the children other than parenting in many cases. So they don't have that [privilege].

Noah Calhoun 27:21 You mentioned that your relationships with your teachers are a big part of your life at school, if you will. Did you participate in any other activities or groups? While attending high school?

Paul Smith 27:33 Yes. I was a member of the Bate school, the first Bate school marching band. I was a drummer. I think if I remember correctly, Mr. Kimball was the first instructor I believe, Dave Kimball, And then we had others but we used to close down Main Street and Main street all the way up to Stanford Avenue. We’d have our homecoming parade. We’d have our floats. The band would be out with majorettes and all that we’d be marching. So, yeah. But I was in the band and I was in lets see what other groups played a little basketball and never a starter you know More of a benchwarmer than anything. I was part of the team in a way. Lets see what else was I in. Woodwork shop made a couple pieces of furniture that I still have. Back then we had different shops we had like automobile repair, carpentry shops. Now we learned all kinds of different things

Sarah Riffe 29:00 It’s a good school I did the I was I applied in Tennessee for Business School and Miss Palmer my. And my twin brother got sick and then [Danny] went to Indiana and my daddy had to put a loan on his house to pay for it cause he went to there to visit and to pay for his hospital. He stayed in they thought he was going to die. So my mother had told me she went up and stayed about three months. And she told me said now you tell me if you buy your clothes for school. I will send you to school to college in Tennessee. But they thought he was gonna die. And Daddy said sis said I had to refinance the house to send him to pay for his hospital expense up there. So I didn't get to go I lost that twice like that, but I don't regret it at all. You know, I love life. I really did. And I have two, I had two sons one died, William but he went to service and come back and work in hospital worked at UK and, and the bedroomed hospital at night you know, but then Sydney went on and finish in KU you know. I don't regret it I seen that they finish school that they went through college and I worked all hard for the pay the expense of the college for them. So I don't regret anything. I missed out on a lot. But yet I've met some lovely people, you know, and some of them are away but they check on me.

Kasey Montgomery 30:54 You said that your boys ended up going to high school in Danville? Right? How was that come?

Sarah Riffe 31:00 Well they went to [a Langlock] in in Gary, Indiana. And then Danny Sydney, came back here when he was in seventh grade and went to Bate. You know, thats ‘fore they changed the school. And then Phillip went to [Jennie Rogers] and finished there and then he went in service. He went to Bowling Green. And he went there to Bowling Green thats where he went to college. And then he went to service, got married and went to service.

Kasey Montgomery 31:41 So your whole family moved back to Danville from Indiana? Speaker 5 31:44 Yeah, yeah, They was kids we lived out here on on what is it out here by Bate school. Out here on what is out there on the farm. On [Lancaster Road] [Snapper Road] [Lancaster] Stanford road

Noah Calhoun 32:04 What year did you guys move back to Danville from Indiana?

Sarah Riffe 32:08 Sydney. Sydney was in the seventh grade when we were back here I don't remember the year right now. Phone rings

[unintelligible] I’m eighty-nine, so I’ve been through a lot. But I took it graceful. You know, I met a lot of lovely people, so I don't complain, I don’t complain.

Kasey Montgomery 32:52 How was going back to Danville? Paul Smith I’m sorry? Kasey Montgomery How was coming back to Danville?

Kasey Montgomery 33:33 Did it look really different, Danville, when you came back?

Sarah Riffe 33:40 Ah, a little bit, a little bit. I took the kids to school, and you know they got along fine. Sydney was kind of shy. But he went on to Eastern, finished at Eastern, you know. Philip went to army after he finished.

Paul Smith 34:13 One of the things that I forgot, that I forgot to mention. We were talking about thing. How were they. Even in the newspaper, Danville advocate as we know it now. Paul Smith 34:48 [unintelligible] come a long way. Still gotta a long way to go. Noah Calhoun 35:12 Mr. Smith, you've mentioned multiple times that sense of community that existed I believe you said on the streets around this area. Paul Smith 35:47 This, the street here, second street, we all grew up here, and this is where we learn to dance, to, as a matter of fact, on one street there was a building called UBL Paul, and a bunch of us got together, we painted that. And we had our own bands on the school made up a band that played there, and we had what they call sock hops. And that was another form of entertainment in this community. There was this, I can't explain the difference between then and now. Other than just say so much violence now. Back then, if you was getting into a fight, as soon as the fight was over, you kind of make up with your friends again. Or if you got into a bad fight, the worst thing you could prolly do was get cut. Now you don't have to even be in a fight and get shot and killed. So I can sympathize and empathize with what you all going through. Because I know it's better and worse and kinda confusing right now. But we didn't have that problem. The community was our support.

Dr. Jeffery Shenton 37:19 Could you tell us more about what a sock hop was like? What did you— tell us what an evening at a sock hop was like.

Paul Smith 37:27 An evening in the Sock Hop was a thing. We would all get all dressed up. Back then I forgot what they called the shoes, but they were brown and white. I can't remember Tom. Dance the night away. It was just a fun time. Mostly it was just a young people there. Wasn’t a lot of outsiders other than that group of teenagers.

Dr. Jeffery Shenton 38:23 Ms. Rifffe, did you go to those kinds of events as well?

Dr. Jeffery Shenton 38:53 And you're saying you worked in other people's homes? Is that right? Where did—

Sarah Riffe 38:57 I worked at homes that kept, took care of kids, children when their parents would go out. You know, sometimes helped serve parties when they went out. And that's when I studied and kept my grades.

Dr. Jeffery Shenton 39:12 You mentioned your family had a farm as well, on Stanford Avenue. Is that true?

Sarah Riffe 39:18 Yeah, the Riffe— Well, yeah, the kids, my children their father, Riffe. Sarah Riffe 39:46 We had tobacco, cattle. Everything. I’d run cattle. Mr. Henderson would help me. Paul Smith 40:25 One thing he was talking about, what kind of jobs did we have. And I think gas was like 32 cents a gallon. Back then. And then it was down right down the street. There was a little grocery store that had deliveries. I used to deliver groceries from that little store. But those, you know, I think the minimum wage, as I recall back then was 60 cents an hour.

Sarah Riffe 41:17 We learned to manage it. Paul Smith You learn to manage it, right.

Kasey Montgomery 51:32 I do have a question. I'm seeing the Colored Notes. I know nowadays we use “Black” a lot. And this, This place is called African American Historical Society. What terms did you use for your community back then?

Kasey Montgomery 52:13 What would you have used to describe your community like words you would have used? Would you have like said “I am a colored person” or “I'm a person of color” “I'm a black person”

Paul Smith 52:25 Back then, “I’m colored.” And now the forms that you had to fill out whether you had to list your race, it was always colored, White, you know, so on. But during that time— and I'm talking in the late 30s, early 40s early 50s, you know. Does that answer your question?

Kasey Montgomery 52:22 Yes, it did.

Sarah Riffe 52:52 I was Sarah. That’s who I was, a person, Sarah. You know, it didn't bother me. You know, I'd go with Black or, you know, negro. Charli Brusnahan 53:44 So you said because you lived in the more rural part. You didn't get to go to the restaurants in town. But you said you cooked a lot, what was your favorite thing to make?

We had to cook food. You know, anything ham, some chicken. We loved to cook, you know, everybody had a job at home. You might go out to work but you still had something to do before you leave the house. Everything wasn’t just on your parents, you know, not just like they are nowadays. But no I enjoyed my life. The older ones took over the younger ones. So everything wasn't just on mother and daddy, but I loved my life out there on Lexington road. It was kind of quiet because we was the only Black in the neighborhood. And my grandfather lived about 14 houses down. So I loved my grandmother, but it was nice. We just didn't have time. My daddy walked to town and catch a cab back. We had cabs then. You know. I lived a life, I don't regret it. I do not. No.

Sarah Riffe 59:01 Oh yeah I (Unintelligible) the chatterbox (Unintelligible).

Dr Jeffrey Shenton 59:46 I was gonna ask if you could talk about your experience in the church if–if the church played a role in–in your upbringing, or what was your relationship to the church like?
