When they, oh to bring the food and everything [Sarah Riffe: Rallies basket meetings?] Basket meetings and you tell about some good food that some of these ladies and gentlemen with (Unintelligible) but they would all bring it and put it out an’ everybody would just come and eat after – after dinner and then have a service, eat in the service. But some of this gospel singing was developed back in those days and in those atmospheres.

Noah Calhoun 1:01:23 So, did you attend the First Baptist Church?

Paul Smith 1:01:26 I attended the Christian church out on the corner. But we all want the benefits of having something in the evenin’, everybody would go there uh, Holiness Church, the Methodist Church whenever it's just y’know, support each other. I think – speaking of churches, this is about me – I think COVID has done a real damage to just when they started the online service online churches a lot of people don't go to church anymore and that's sad. That's really bad.

Sarah Riffe 1:02:03 They have Zoom and Facebook, eh look at it. I'm just, I’m’a gonna go to church y’know and we enjoyed that. I played at the church today (Unintelligible) my sister played first when she left home, I took over playing out there. [Kasey Montgomery: Like, the piano?] Mhm, and when I went to Gary Naylor, I played up there, y’know.

Paul Smith 1:02:29 Her son’s a great musician. Yeah, and he plays with the Christian church all around town. Sydney.

Noah Calhoun 1:02:37 Is there any specific music you guys remember standing out from the church services or church events in your childhood?

Paul Smith 1:02:45 In a particular music? [Noah Calhoun: Yeah] Some gospels back then, to me, maybe because (Unintelligible) Gospel songs had a name they really was not rap it was good Gospel songs to move something in you, y’know. I know rap does to you all. It’s altogether different. The words have meanings “nearer my god to thee” you know that meant somethin’ to me. Uh, “I'll fly away” to me, I can see myself but goodbye day I’m flyin’ outta here y’know? Those kinds of songs touched me deeply and I know that's different from the music today (Unintelligible) each generation have they own type a music. My parents thought we'd gone crazy when we got to (Unintelligible) and all that kind of stuff but they (Unintelligible)

Noah Calhoun 1:03:59 Kind of on that subject of music in your generation, what music did you – you listen to with your friends or other people outside of necessarily church environment?

Paul Smith 1:04:08 Oh I love jazz. Fats Domino, B.B. King, Lloyd Price, y’know that kinda, that group. They – they were great singers. But uh, jazz I like. I just – all kinds of jazz. What you was askin’, did that answer your question, basically? [Noah Calhoun: Yes, yeah it did] We uh, we used to love the jitterbug, and all the bass – all that type a music. And that's what you asked about the sock hop, that’s what we did a lot of – the jitterbug.

Sara Riffe 1:05:01 Don’t look at me. [All laughing] I lived in the country, we didn’t have (Unintelligible) church, y’know, and uh, y’know, grocery store shopping and stuff like that. But I worked, I was like my Daddy, I love to work. I like to have my own (Unintelligible). So, I didn't do a lot of (Unintelligible) my sister didn't have a car, my sister, one of my sisters finally eventually got a car and she drove all the time y’know. My mother could drive, but she was so busy and farmin’, workin’, and cookin’ and taking care of ch– my mother took care of kids all over the (Unintelligible) country out there on Lexington Road. Fed ‘em, kept ‘em, some days the people just let them out of the car, and just leave and she never know how many she’s gonna have for supper. Sometimes she keep ‘em a week. And–and when she called people to–to table, and some were missin’ they said they were the parents come by and pick ‘em up. They wouldn't even say thank you or nothin’. But she fed ‘em, took care of ‘em when they were sick. You know, so we always had a bunch of people around us – chirren around us, y’know, people come up th’road, and want a place to stay, uh, that they had a problem, and Mama would take ‘em and keep ‘em until they got on they foot and leave, y’know. So, (Unintelligible) we was a big family. We just errybody worked ‘n helped ‘n took care of ‘em – fed ‘em, y’know. My mother said when she got sick, she'd have a lot of people here because she took care of so many children and parents didn’t say thank you or nothin’ but she kept themv– fed ‘em. I’s seen her many times stay up at night with ‘em when they sick and stay with them. So we was taught that – to take care of people and see to them, y’know.

Noah Calhoun 1:07:02 Would you say that kind of hospitality that your mother extended to people was something that was very common in that whole community? Or what do you think [Sara Rife: No, mostly her. Just her.] Just her?

Sarah Riffe 1:07:13 She took ‘em and y’know other people take and – leave they chirren y’know take ‘em and – let ‘em out the car ‘cause know she’d be there. Mama–Mama stayed with us kind of (Unintelligible). You know, she was always involved at PTA, everything. But she was there at home to take and see to her chirren. And errybody else’s chirren. ‘N it made no difference. It didn’t. She’d love ‘em, dress ‘em, make us clothes. And we’d think that we’s gon get to wear them, ‘n if somebody came along and needed it, she’d give it to them. Y’know, this, that’s the type of person she was and she taught us to be the same way. I don't mind and I don't resent it. Um, it's been a lot of good people.

Paul Smith 1:08:02 Now I know you all got a lot of information (Unintelligible) and other folks. We just want to hope we were able to shed light on some of the highlights of the things that were important to us. (Unintelligible) we could never thank the teachers and administration at Bate High School for all they did for us, as well as our churches, (Unintelligible) civic leaders. Really wonderful.

Dr. Jeffrey Shenton 1:08:32 Well, we really want to thank you for all the information that you've given us today and answering our questions. Are there–are there–is there anything that we haven't covered that you really wanted to cover that you would like to say before we wrap up?

Paul Smith 1:08:48 No [Dr. Jeffrey Shenton: That’s okay]

Sarah Riffe 1:08:48 I have just wondered, because when I came back here, my son, I took him to (Unintelligible) for music every, every Saturday for study. And when I came back here, I went to Bate School one to they had changed the school, mixed the school and they gave him to Danville High, they said I went up there and asked and they said they didn't have any of the instruments so Ion know what they did with ‘em. When we was coming up, they didn't have to buy instruments, the schools had ‘em, y’know they donated when they left Bate and–and but they said they didn't know where. So I went to Centre because Sydney, Sydney played accordion and they had accordion up there, and he played and they said they didn't have a accordion band here. So you had to get rid of them. (Unintelligible) They had accordion in the bands and it get written out.

Dr. Jeffrey Shenton 1:09:53 Mr. Hughes, did you have any questions? [Michael Hughes: Uh no] Before we wrapped up?

Michael Hughes 1:09:55 I just knew I've been waiting on this interview for [All laugh] over a year because I know these are special people y’know and I appreciate you guys you know that I don't have to tell you how much I appreciate. And uh, thanks for coming in.

Sarah Riffe 1:10:12 Yeah (Unintelligible) My daddy used to walk, your auntie used to work down there by Shaw Bridge, and he used to walk down there and pick her up at night, bring her up to the house and she stayed up there.

Michael Hughes 1:10:26 She’s one of the people that knew my mother when she was young [Sarah Riffe: Yeah I did. I met her when he’d bring her up and she’d stay at night] told me some great stories like that and I knew when – the moment she said that I said, We gotta get her. [All laugh] I know she got some stories to tell! And of course Paul, I knew ‘bout, I just – how many – How long have we actually known each other? Not a long time. [Paul Smith: Eight, ten years maybe?] Yeah, and you know, so, I knew this was one of the interviews that we had to have on–in our archives.

Paul Smith 1:11:00 Appreciate it. I think he had a question?

Noah Calhoun 1:11:02 I did. Is there any like last stories or last memories that you would just like to share from that time period from your childhood growing up in that area?

Paul Smith 1:11:17 Last stories? Uh, oh, I guess if I had to share a last story that would be how much that I really enjoyed my childhood even though it was segregated. And I can’t thank the people, the village that helped me pick up what I have today. So, f you have an opportunity to reach out to somebody, do it.

Dr. Jeffrey Shenton 1:11:48 I have to ask before we wrap up I want to did you know each other when you were when you were little? [Paul Smith: Yes] You did? [Sarah Riffe: Well] [All laugh]

Paul Smith 1:11:59 We uh, grew up more or less together. Same school, same church. Family was always friends my mom and her.

Sarah Riffe 1:12:11 His sister and my sister went to the same place together – went to school together in Danville at Bate, y’know. [Paul Smith: That’s why] Huh?

Paul Smith 1:12:23 That’s why our mama’s was best friends.

Sarah Riffe 1:12:25 Friends. Same church, they went to church.

Paul Smith 1:12:31 We’ve had different mates, but they passed on ‘n we got back together. [All laugh]

Dr. Jeffrey Shenton 1:12:40 Well, thank you both so much for your time today. Thank you. [Noah Calhoun, Kasey Montgomery, Charli Brusnahan: Thank you]
waltersmith:
Walter Smith

“back in the day, the things that we went through, the black community, that we went through, you know, the prejudice and segregation, things like that.”

Transcript

Riley: Hello. My name is Riley Parker, and today I'm interviewing Walter Smith, who is a resident of Danville, Kentucky. I'm here with Rothavy Kong and Lauren Silva. Today is January, 23 2025 and we're recording this in the Danville Boyle County African American Historical Society. Today we will be talking about Walter's experience during urban renewal in Danville, Kentucky. So Walter, can you tell us about what it was, what it's like living in Danville, your experiences with it?

Walter: Today’s?

Riley: Yeah.

Walter: For today is way more than yesterday's. You know, back in the day, the things that we went through, the black community, that we went through, you know, the prejudice and segregation, things like that.

Rothavy: Yes, Walter, can you tell us a little bit about your family?

Walter: My family?

Rothavy: Yes.

Walter: My family was the things that they told me that they went through, the trials and tribulations that you know, they went through. What do you mean my family, like my mother and them?

Rothavy: Yes like…

Walter: Well, my mother, passed when I was 10 years old. My brother and sisters went to live with one of her sisters. I was raised by two of my uncles. So I mean.

Rothavy: Were you like an only child, or do you have any siblings or?

Walter: Yes, I have sisters and brothers.

Rothavy: What do your sisters and brothers do? What do your sisters and brothers do? Like, what are their careers and stuff?

Walter: My brother, my brother’s at UPS. My sisters are nurses, and you know.

Lauren: Could you tell us a little bit about how it was growing up in Danville?

Walter: Growing up in Danville? Okay, growing up in Danville, back in the segregation days, you know, it's like the movie theaters we, the blacks had to sit up, up top. And some of the restaurants we were served in the back. Back of the drugstore had a black section. You know, went through a lot of racial it was very racial climate. You know, the N-word, it was used a lot, like that, you know. Bate school, I went to Bate school up until the fifth grade when, when they integrated. So we all had to, most of my friends had spread out. So when they, most of my friends went to the white schools, they felt inferior in the classroom with a bunch of whites. But me, I've always been I uh, educated to where I wasn't intimidated. I always had, you know, achieved scholastically high-grade point averages and stuff like that. So I didn't let that intimidate me, but you know, to go out like me and my uncle to go out if I wanted him to be called the N-word by somebody, you know, just stuff like that. It was really hard, you know, so really hard back then.

Lauren: Could you tell us a little bit more about your experience at Bate school, and then how it changed after the schools became integrated?

Walter: Oh Bate school?

Walter: We had, well, it was a restful climate to where we had the all black teachers, where they knew my mom, you know. We went to church with most of the teachers, you know, it was more of a, you know, personal. But it seemed like when we went to the, when we integrated, and the white teachers, I'm just, I'm just being real. I'm real. I'm real. The white teachers it’s like, they didn't take to us, like the black teachers did. They, you know, it was more, you know, like they put us in a box. But I wouldn't, I refuse to be put in that box, you know. Straight A's, I never made a B in grade school or high school, so I let them know that, I was taught under my roof at the household a lot of things, so it was hard. Some and some of my classmates, that's in my age group. When they went to the sixth grade, they was held back because of the fact that they felt intimidated, you know, in the classroom, but not Walter. You know, so.

Rothavy: So what was your favorite, like, things to do in school? What were the activities that you did in school? What were your the favorite activities that you did in school?

Walter: Band. Into band, band, and also, you know, communication, mass communication, it was my major in college, mass communication, broadcasting.

Riley: Going back to your family, you said you had a brother and sister, right? What was your experience growing up with him? Like, how did you guys interact? And are you still close or…

Walter: What I mentioned was, my mom passed when I was turning 11. My brother and sister was younger, so they went to live with one of her sisters, and I was raised by two of her brothers. I was raised by two of my uncles. So, I mean, we're still, you know, we're still, you know, two blocks away. So you know, we're still close, but that, that was the point of my mom passed. And when, you know, when I was young.

Rothavy: Can you share a specific story from your school days that has like, taught you an important lesson today? Like, a specific story from your school days that you, like, taught you an important lesson today.

Walter: In high school?

Rothavy: Yes, in high school, when you went to Bate or something

Walter: Well, I mean, I don't really know. There's a lot of instances where I just learned to get along with everybody myself. I was one of those types that you know, to know me, was to know me and I got along with everybody you know, just that was the thing with me. Helping other people in the class with all the subjects. I never had a problem with the subjects. I, you know, helping each other and, you know, meeting people that they thought that you didn't care for them and then when you got to socializing, and, you know, you found out that, you know, everything was kosher.

Lauren: Could you tell us a little bit about the places that you went to growing up here in Danville, some of the businesses that you saw when you were growing up?

Walter: About what?

Lauren: Some of the places that you went to when you were growing up in Danville, and then some of the businesses that were here.

Walter: That we weren't allowed to go, is that what you told me?

Lauren: Yes, some of the places that you went to that was part of your daily life.

Walter: Well, Hub Frankel, up there on the corner of, I don't know what to call it now, and like to say, the movie theater, some of the restaurants that we was allowed to go in. You talking about when I was growing up?

Lauren: Yeah.

Water: Some of the restaurants, you know, it was, there's a place called the Bun Boy where, like I said, we had to get, we had to pull in the back, the blacks wasn't allowed to go in. They had all black cooks and one day me and my two uncles went down. I was a little boy. I said, Well, how come we came? You know, when we seen the white people going in, they said that we wasn’t allowed to go in and, and that kind of took one damage and. Like, say, the movie theater, we had to go up. We had to set up top Begley George Stewart, had a black section and you know, all that kind of, you know back then that you said, “Why? Why? Why?” you know, so we just had odd places that we went, that we felt welcome.

Rothavy: Mm. Can you tell us more about the Bun Boy that you mentioned?

Walter: Bun Boy?

Rothavy: Yeah Bun Boy.

Walter: Bun Boy restaurant downstairs. You know it was, it was just a place where, like I said, we had to get served and order in the back. You get out of your car and go and order, go back to the car and then, and they hollerin' about it, but we wasn’t allowed to go in. But I asked my uncle, I said, “they got black cooks”. They had Black cooks and, you know, back then it was just a racial climate, you know, a racial climate, you.

Riley: Can you tell me a little bit, or a little bit, about your college experiences after you graduated high school?

Walter: Yeah, I went to Eastern Kentucky University, major in mass communication broadcasting. Graduated from Eastern, I join fraternity with, we had to have a, to pledge you had to have at least a 3.5 grade point average, you know, Omega Psi Phi fraternity, which I represent. So it was a real good experience.

Rothavy: So you said that you major in mass communication broadcasting. What motivates you to, like, choose that major and like going through that field?

Walter: I was always into back then, music and stuff like that. Y'all remember, old man. Y'all probably don't remember Walter Cronkite. Okay, they threw the name my name Walter, so my nickname that my friends told me was Cronkite and stuff like that. But that's just always been my uh, you know, interest.

Lauren: Could you tell us about your experience with urban renewal here in Danville?

Walter: Urban renewal?

Lauen: Yeah.

Walter: Urban renewal was a travesty. They came through the black neighborhoods and bought house for way less money than the property was the gate. They stole the black people's property. They come up through Second Street and stole their property, and they spread it out the black, the black community. It was just a breakup of the black men. Urban renewal was a rip-off, and I don't see why the NAACP or somebody didn't step in, stop them. They stole the black man's property. And back then they, I guess the Blacks didn't have no voice. Yeah, urban renewal was a rip-off.

Rothavy: So… okay, I’m sorry.

Walter: People still talked about it.

Rothavy: So that you mentioned that Danville has gone through like, you know, urban renewal, and has gone through, like, a lot of changes. So how, how have you seen these changes affect the community and also, like your family yourself as well?

Walter: Well, it just affected where they broke up the black community and spreaded the black community out. Stole the blacks’ property, you know, gave them less money than the property was worth, you know, and it hurt a lot of families. Hurt a lot of families, urban renewal. And people behind urban renewal was lining their pockets, lining their pockets well, you know. But how can the Black community say something when the white’s power? You know? Because around here you don't see no minorities on the Chamber of Commerce, the board, or the banks or anything. You know, so, so it's, it's, it's like that. I, when I was in high school, I, I was the first one of all the, through high school, most of us tried to start a Black Student Union. And I hate to brag, I was the one that started the First Black Student Union at Danville. I was the one to start that wrote up, uh, students and everything I got that started, went through the Board of Education and went through everything. They didn't want it to be called the Black Student Union. They wanted to call the Student Union. I said “No, we’re gonna stick with the Black Student Union”, because the whites had the chess club, FHA, and all that blacks was coming to me, said, “Man, how come we don't have nothing”. I told them that when the homeroom bell rings to meet me and don't, do not go to the homeroom, meet me in the lobby, and that's when it started. I've just always been that way. I fight for the rights that I see fit. I've never been afraid to speak up. I don't care where I'm at.

Lauren: Can you tell us a little bit about how creating this Black Student Union affected your life and, and your community? Part 2: (15:51-30:43)

Walter: Well, that when we started the Black Student Union, it was that we, the blacks, were very we had mass attendance and everything, but it died off, you know? Because here's the thing, I grew up around my people and some of the, my nephews and all them now, they grew up staying all hanging with the other race, you know. So they didn't see, you know, like I was at a barbershop a few years ago, sitting in there, and this little young dude said, “Man Martin Luther King didn't do a blank thing for me”. And men, this old man looked at each other. See, they don't learn stuff like that. I hate that they're gonna teach black history in school because of the fact that I feel that teaching black history, some teachers find out that there are relatives down the line with some of the slave masters. It's been proven, and I don't see why it's not taught in school. They know about all the white history, but I was taught all that. I got mass of bass collection of Black History at my house, and when I was young, well, I was taught under my roof. I'm buried in the hospital on my Black history.

Lauren: Since we're already talking about history and education, a little bit, could you tell us a little bit about how your education changed when you were still in Bates and then after your schools became integrated? How was that shift for you?

Walter: How was it what from the black school?

Lauren: Yeah, and then when the schools became integrated, how did your education change?

Walter: Well in the block I was learning about my history. But when I went to the white schools, I would bring up things, and the teacher said “No, Walter, we don't want to talk about that. We won't talk about that”. And every day, every day, I would make it a point by throwing some black history. “Oh no, Walter, we don't talk about that. We don't talk about that”. I used to go home and they said, “Well, Walter, you know us the way it is”, but you know so

Riley: How did you see the community in your school change with the creation of the Black Student Union?

Walter: The community?

Riley: Yeah

Walter: It was no, we did a lot of events around Danville that the parents were saying, “Oh, wow”. They were seeing, you know, they were very proud. And the Blacks at Centre College back then, back in I graduated in 73 the rocks over center, they would come up and help us out a lot. So it was very you know, achievement.

Rothavy: How would you describe the cultural and like social atmosphere of your community when you were growing up?

Walter: Yeah, the culture

Rothavy: Of like, of Danville, of your like community when you were growing up, like the social atmosphere, or like the cultural atmosphere?

Walter: What was it like? Well, I mean, it was like we had on our side, we had our, you know, the church. You know, people come to church and like we just had our environment. You know, we just had our environment where we did. I didn't realize we didn't really interact because they didn't want us to interact them. So we just had our environment, the clubs and, you know, guidelines and things we'd have family reunions, you know? So we just created our own activities.

Lauren: How would you how did your environment change after experiencing urban renewal?

Walter: Well, you know, like to say the neighborhood. Everybody like neighborhood, they split up. So everybody just spread it out. And, you know, it was something to get used to. Because, you know, the people that used to be, you know, we had the community. Everybody was split up. The people really didn't care where they, you know, it's like some of the white communities are gonna bring them over here, you know, stuff like that. So you have to just keep them in that, in that small area, you know, it was, I think was bad time, you know, back then. But we survived.

Rothavy: What was your favorite place to like hang out with your friends when you were a child, before the urban renewal, like the places we had

Walter: We had the projects and we had our parts. You know, go play basketball, the swimming pools and places like that. We had a place man on the farm and in town, we go play baseball and tell us a big gathering, things like that. We made all the fun we got

Rothavy: So, like, during your time, were there a lot of, like, black-owned businesses? like, in the past, like…

Walter: yeah, that was a black-owned, you know, black-owned businesses, yeah.

Rothavy: Can you tell us, like, one about, like, one black-owned businesses like, during that time, like, what was, what were those?

Walter: There were restaurants and bicycle shops and the small grocery stores. One more uncles owned a grocery store.

Lauren: Did you have an experience with the black Masonic Lodge or not? Did you have any experience with the black Masonic Lodge or not really?

Walter: Yeah. When my uncle was the national secretary, he was the national area secretary of the… Masonic Lodge? Yes, yes. When my uncles, that raised me, was the national secretary, where all the men would come up and at the house and pay the dues. But I never did join masonics. But yeah, they had placed down there on Second Street that Darby know about their building. Stole their building from them, Masonic lodge. Yeah, several of them. My uncles was a Masonic (unintelligible). No Masonic lines. Yeah, that was one of the biggest Black organizations in Danville. They held for every 14 they had a mass picnic guy that everybody around the community ever would come in.

Riley: So you mentioned you lived with your uncles growing up. How have they impacted any of your values or beliefs or anything that you have now? Like shaped you as a person.

Walter: They was very hard workers. There was meticulous dresses. They taught me about they taught me a lot of times that lot of values in life growing up, they taught me a lot. You know, my two uncles. You know, because my sisters and brother that was younger, so I was turning 11, so I've been with them, but they taught me a lot the work values and things like, may they be rested in peace.

Rothavy: So you mentioned that you're like, you have, like, younger sisters and brothers. So like, as, like, like, as, like, oldest child. What would you say, like, what were like the lessons or value in the past that has shaped who you are today?

Walter: Excuse me?

Rothavy: Like, what were the like lessons that you've learned in the past that has shaped who you are today?

Walter: Well, my advice and she told me to always take care of like and and I'd still in them like the school work I helped, you know, brought them up in school work times and days they turned out really good, you know. So…

Lauren: so going back to the Black Student Union, did you experience any pushback when you were trying to create the organization? And if so, what were some of the experiences that you had with that?

Walter: Push back? Well, we had a lot of pushbacks. You know, were like, I say they they wanted, they didn't want the Black to be put on the Student Union and and they already had a student union there. So we, me, me and the ones that helped organize that we had a tough fight going back and forth to the Board of Education, the principals and all that. But we pushed it. We really pushed it through and some of the older ones that had graduated from Danville that tried to do it, you know, they was very proud of the fact that we actually got it started. You know, because from day one, I was used to give up, you know. So, you know, they thought that they would throw a lot of things at us, curve balls, but you know, I knew how to catch so I'm just a part. I'm not scared to stand up for what I want.

Rothavy: Talking about the Black Student reunion. What motivates you to, like, start the, you know, the the association or something…

Walter: The Black Student Union?

Rothavy: Yes

Walter: But as I stated, Blacks were saying, well, we don't have none, you know. They had the FHA farmers, you know, and (unintelligible). They just had a lot of organizations that really said, “Man, how come we can't have, like, a state?” I said, “Well, you see, maybe we should start a Black Student Union”. You know, when home bell rang, home bell rings, meet me in the lobby. Will refuse to go to the homeroom. So a bunch of us met me in the lobby. It was like a small protest. Principal called me in the office, and, you know, from there, you know, they thought that, you know what, we can't have is and I said, “Yes, we can”, you know, so they seem no fear.

Riley: Can you tell us about how your experiences like or like, what experiences you had with your fraternity whenever you went to Eastern Kentucky?

Walter: My fraternity, you mean…

Riley: Like, just how it impacted you, and what activities you did there.

Walter: We did, we do a lot of social events. You know, social events during the fraternity was on my mind before I went to Eastern because the Black principal at Bates school before the invader, he was Omega so I joined fraternity, I would say, you know, I'd say you have to have a 3.5 to (unintelligible). But I said, like, I'm good in English, math, like that. Some brother might be, you know, we helped each other out. And, you know, it's plus I'm a member of the grad chapter of grad, I'm still involved with the grad chapter, you know, so we, I'm in the mentorship over in Lexington, you know, we go, we go in the grade schools and mentor young boys. You know, we have a lot of lot events. Town shows that we do a lot of events.

Rothavy: Can you tell us about your experience when you were in the fraternity and when you were a mentor to the young boys and stuff? Part 3:

Walter: Yeah, like I said, in the mentorship, We [have] a lot of single households, you know mothers that and we take, we have other boys. We do things with, show them how to dress, tie a tie. We do a lot of events with the kids, you know, mentoring with the grades, you know, things like some that's kind of having trouble we straighten them out. You know, we just, we just dive on.

Lauren: Could you tell us a little bit about your working career after college?

Walter: After college?

Lauren: Yeah, after college.

Walter: I worked at Philips Lighting for 30 years. I was in process, and 30 years and, well, now I'm retired. So I help, I do ushering service. I just ushering service at the church. Now I'm retired. I devote a lot of my time now to the grad chapter, my fraternity.

Rothavy: So you did mention like going to church. So I like a lot of like about churches. So what is like the role of church, like, plays in your life?

Walter: Church?

Rothavy: Like church, yes, church. Like, how would you experience, you know, going to church? And like, yeah…

Walter: Yeah, I devote, you know, a lot of time to church. As a little boy, church every Sunday. You know, every Sunday, and I just like give my time to the Lord.

Lauren: Have you always lived here in Danville?

Walter: No.

Lauren: Oh, can you tell us a little bit more about the places you have lived?

Walter: Frankfort. I've lived in Frankfort. And back and forth to Louisville, you know, but Danville is, this is why I worked here. So I was mainly here.

Lauren: And when did you, when did you return to Danville?

Walter: Like in 83.

Lauren: Okay, and how has the sense of community here in Danville changed from when you were a kid to now?

Walter: Oh, it's a major, major change. At least we can walk through the front door to a lot of places, you know. Like, you know, whether we feel welcome or not, but now you know, it's just changed drastically.

Riley: You mentioned living in Frankfort. How was the atmosphere, like the social atmosphere, different than it was here at Danville?

Walter: It wasn’t that different. Frankfurt after I left college. I lived down there for a few years. It was, but the same, it was no different.

Rothavy: So you did mention that you went to college and, like you did mass communication. So were there any challenges that you had to face when you were in, like, your college years?

Walter: Challenges?

Rothavy: Yes, just challenges you faced.

Walter: Not really. I mean, everything come natural. My, you know, school work and grades, didn't have problems with grades and nothing like that. You know, just…

Riley: Can you, can you share any specific events or stories that help shape how you think about or how you view the world and society? Just kind of as a whole?

Walter: Excuse me?

Riley: Can you share any stories or events in your life that helped change how you view society or the world, just in general?

Walter: Uh, okay. No, not really, just not really. I don't want to expand on some things that I'm feeling, so I just keep it in.

Riley: Thank you.

Rothavy: Would you say that your experience going to Bates School and your friend's experience, like, going to the like, White school, like, how are like, what are the difference and like, what were the challenges that you say that they faced there?

Walter: From high school to…

Rothavy: Your friends, you said that they went to the White school. What, what would you say the challenges that they faced there?

Walter: Well, like I say, that was it by being around all peoples, it was more personal, personable. But anybody like say, I was going from the fifth to the sixth. So when they integrated, when they integrated, the ones, you know, my age group, they went to different schools, and they just felt they felt intimidated by sitting in a class with all the Whites, you know. Even though they knew, like knew the answer, or whatever, they just felt they put a wall around themselves, you know. Like I said, with me, I knew, I mean, every answer I wanted to let somebody else answer. I refused to be intimidated. And when my test papers laid on the table, it was all A, A plus plus, you know. And so I intimidated my fellow students of the other race. I was the smart blank, smart A, because I refuse to be unknowledgeable when I knew I had knowledge.

Prof. Shenton: Growing up, did you have any thoughts about Centre College? Or did your family, your uncles, talk about Centre College at all? Or did that even enter into your, the the way in which you thought about Danville?

Walter: Centre College? No, I was, like attendance, just no.

Prof. Shenton: I mean, thoughts about it, for attendance, whatever.

Walter: Well, back then, back, you know, it wasn't none of us walking around over there, on the campus, you know. We'd ride through, and I'd ask, my uncles I don't see none of us walk around nobody. It was about in the maybe early 70s, you know, when we start. And so Centre wouldn’t, you know, just ride through when a lot of us over there, even though I could have went and excelled, but I didn't send it. I didn't have no interest in Centre.

Prof. Shenton: You did say you had some interaction with some of the first black Students that were at Centre College. What was that interaction like? And how did that, did that change your perspective on it? Or what did you, what was that interaction like?

Walter: like, oh yeah, back in the 70s, when I hit high school, I know Raymond Burris is one of them that would go by. They uh, they helped us with the Black Student Union. They helped out a whole lot. We go over there. That's when we'd go right on campus, and they'd come up to Danville High School. They would, the Blacks over there would come down to the project, shoot basketball and stuff. They interacted with us. So…

Prof. Shenton: We interviewed Dr. Burke a few years ago here for this. I'm glad that you're you're telling, he actually talked quite a bit about that too, so it was meaningful to him as well. So thank you for sharing, sharing that that part of it. Thinking also about the future of Danville. What do you think now, so you've lived through decades here in Danville. What do you think Danville will look like? Or how will it be different in 15 or 20 years? Do you think?

Walter: Well I hope it keeps progressing. You know, it's a nice place to live, Danville, something keeps progressing, you know. But like I mentioned early, you know, there's people on Chamber of Commerce, they don't want to run the communities. We don't, I've never seen the minority on we do have a Black mayor, but on the bank boards, there's no large no minorities. Chamber of Commerce, no minority. So I hope all that you know, we've been designed on the physical course as well.

Rothavy: So you said that you've lived in Frankfort and Louisville and also Danville. What would you say, like your experience, like, in Frankfort, Louisville, and like, compared to Danville?

Walter: It wouldn't, you know. When I left Eastern, I moved to Frankfort, you know, K State, Kentucky, State University. It was all Blacks here, so I lived down there. It was about, I mean, it was, you know, about the same, I mean, you know it's, but no. I say, you familiar with Kentucky State University? Kentucky State, it definitely just be all-Black school. So, you know, we always feel a little comfortable.
